html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 钦定四库全书 - Formal title indicating the work was officially compiled by imperial decree.
  • 子部十二 - Classification within the imperial library system, indicating the work belongs to the 'Miscellaneous' category.
  • 小説家二【异闻之属】 - Further classification indicating the work falls under the 'Tales of the Strange' subcategory.
  • 提要 - Summary or preface providing an overview of the work.
  • 奉勅监修 - Indicates the work was commissioned and supervised by imperial order.
  • 同修者 - Refers to the co-editors or collaborators on the project.
  • 表进 - To present a written document to the emperor.
  • 雕板印行 - Refers to the process of carving wooden blocks for printing and then printing the work.
  • 轶闻事 - Refers to unusual or extraordinary events and stories.
  • 僻笈遗文 - Refers to obscure or forgotten texts.
  • 渊海 - Metaphor for a vast and deep body of knowledge.
  • 言者谓 - Refers to someone who said or argued.
  • 非后学所急 - Means 'not urgent for later scholars.'
  • 收板贮之 - To collect and store the printing blocks.
  • 总目不载 - Indicates that the work is not listed in the general catalog.
  • 号为博洽 - Refers to someone known for their extensive knowledge.
  • 未见其书 - Indicates that the person has not seen the book.
  • 虽多谈神怪 - Indicates that the work contains many stories about supernatural phenomena.
  • 采摭繁富 - Means 'richly compiled from many sources.'
  • 名物典故 - Refers to proper nouns, historical events, and cultural references.
  • 词章家恒所取资 - Means 'constantly used as a resource by writers.'
  • 皇览 - A historical text compiled during the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 三辅决录 - A historical record of the Three Auxiliaries (regions around the capital).
  • 晋阳秋 - A historical text about the Jin dynasty.
  • 晋中兴书 - A historical text about the revival of the Jin dynasty.
  • 齐春秋 - A historical text about the Qi dynasty.
  • 益部耆旧传 - A historical text about the elderly and distinguished people of the Yi region.
  • 汝南先贤传 - A historical text about the distinguished people of the Ruyan region.
  • 防稽先贤传 - A historical text about the distinguished people of the Kuaiji region.
  • 古文语 - Refers to ancient texts or sayings.
  • 琴清英 - A text about music and its refinement.
  • 世语 - A text about the language and customs of the world.
  • 符子 - A text by Fu Zi, a historical figure.
  • 金楼子 - A text by Xiao Tong, a prince of the Liang dynasty.
  • 诸书世所不传者 - Refers to various books that are not widely known or transmitted.
  • 断简残编 - Refers to fragments or incomplete texts.
  • 尚间存其什一 - Means 'still retains a small fraction.'
  • 尤足贵也 - Means 'especially valuable.'
  • 恭校上 - A formal expression indicating that the work was carefully proofread and presented to the emperor.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 钦定四库全书 - A massive imperial library project commissioned by Emperor Qianlong during the Qing dynasty.
  • 子部十二 - The 'Miscellaneous' category in the imperial library system, which includes works on various subjects not fitting into the main categories.
  • 小説家二【异闻之属】 - The 'Tales of the Strange' subcategory, which includes works on supernatural, mythical, and unusual events.
  • 提要 - A common feature in Chinese bibliographies, providing a brief summary of a work.
  • 奉勅监修 - Indicates the high status of the work, as it was commissioned and supervised by the emperor.
  • 同修者 - The co-editors of the work, who were prominent scholars and officials of the time.
  • 表进 - A formal process of presenting a completed work to the emperor.
  • 雕板印行 - The advanced printing technology used during the Song dynasty, which allowed for the mass production of books.
  • 轶闻事 - Reflects the interest in extraordinary and unusual events in Chinese literature.
  • 僻笈遗文 - Highlights the value of preserving and studying obscure and forgotten texts.
  • 渊海 - A metaphor for the vastness and depth of knowledge contained in the work.
  • 言者谓 - Indicates that there were differing opinions or debates about the work.
  • 非后学所急 - Reflects a practical concern about the relevance of the work to later scholars.
  • 收板贮之 - Indicates the physical preservation of the work through the storage of printing blocks.
  • 总目不载 - Suggests that the work may have been overlooked or undervalued in some catalogs.
  • 号为博洽 - Refers to the reputation of scholars for their extensive knowledge.
  • 未见其书 - Indicates that some scholars may not have had access to the work.
  • 虽多谈神怪 - Indicates that the work contains many supernatural stories, which were common in Chinese literature.
  • 采摭繁富 - Reflects the extensive research and compilation involved in the work.
  • 名物典故 - Highlights the importance of cultural and historical references in the work.
  • 词章家恒所取资 - Indicates that the work was a valuable resource for writers.
  • 皇览 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
  • 三辅决录 - A historical record that was a source for the work.
  • 晋阳秋 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
  • 晋中兴书 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
  • 齐春秋 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
  • 益部耆旧传 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
  • 汝南先贤传 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
  • 防稽先贤传 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
  • 古文语 - Refers to ancient texts that were sources for the work.
  • 琴清英 - A text that was a source for the work.
  • 世语 - A text that was a source for the work.
  • 符子 - A text that was a source for the work.
  • 金楼子 - A text that was a source for the work.
  • 诸书世所不传者 - Refers to books that were rare or obscure sources for the work.
  • 断简残编 - Highlights the value of preserving fragments of texts.
  • 尚间存其什一 - Indicates that even small fragments are valuable.
  • 尤足贵也 - Emphasizes the importance of the work.
  • 恭校上 - Indicates the formal and careful process of proofreading and presenting the work to the emperor.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅 - Titles and names of officials responsible for compiling or overseeing a project. '总纂官' means 'Chief Compiler' or 'Chief Editor.' The use of '【臣】' indicates that these individuals are officials addressing their emperor.
  • 总校官【臣】陆费墀 - '总校官' means 'Chief Proofreader' or 'Chief Reviser,' another official title.
  • 钦定四库全书 - '钦定' means 'imperial decree' or 'ordained by the emperor,' and '四库全书' refers to the 'Four Treasuries,' a massive collection of books commissioned by the Qing Dynasty.
  • 神仙一 - '神仙' means 'immortals' or 'deities,' and '一' here likely indicates the first volume or section of a series on immortals.
  • 生而能言 - Literally 'born speaking,' an idiom indicating that the person was exceptionally wise or knowledgeable from birth.
  • 指李树曰以此为我姓 - Literally 'pointed to the plum tree and said this will be my surname,' an idiom suggesting that the person chose their surname based on a sign or event.
  • 下三皇时为金阙帝君 - '下三皇' refers to another set of mythical rulers, and '金阙帝君' means 'Imperial Lord of the Golden Gate,' another high-ranking deity.
  • 伏羲时为郁华子 - '伏羲' is a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, and '郁华子' is a title or epithet for a deity or sage.
  • 神农时为九灵老子 - '神农' is another legendary figure, and '九灵老子' means 'Laozi, the Nine Spirits,' indicating a divine or semi-divine status.
  • 黄帝时为广成子 - '黄帝' is the legendary Yellow Emperor, and '广成子' is a title or epithet for a sage or deity.
  • 颛顼时为赤精子 - '颛顼' is a legendary ruler, and '赤精子' means 'Red-Blooded Son,' another title for a deity.
  • 帝喾时为禄图子 - '帝喾' is another legendary ruler, and '禄图子' means 'Son of Fortune,' another title for a deity.
  • 尧时为务成子 - '尧' is the legendary Emperor Yao, and '务成子' is a title or epithet for a sage or deity.
  • 夏禹时为真行子 - '夏禹' is the legendary Emperor Yu, and '真行子' means 'True Walker,' another title for a deity.
  • 殷汤时为锡则子 - '殷汤' is the legendary Emperor Tang of Shang, and '锡则子' means 'Son of Granting,' another title for a deity.
  • 文王时为文邑先生 - '文王' is the legendary King Wen of Zhou, and '文邑先生' means 'Mr. of the Literary Village,' another title for a sage or scholar.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 老子 - Laozi is a central figure in Daoism, traditionally credited with writing the *Tao Te Ching*. He is often depicted as a philosopher and sage who achieved immortality.
  • 木公 - '木公' is a title for a deity, possibly related to wood or trees. In Daoist mythology, '木公' is sometimes associated with the God of Wood or the God of Trees.
  • 广成子 - '广成子' is a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with Daoist teachings. He is said to have lived for thousands of years and taught Laozi.
  • 黄安 - Without further context, it is unclear who '黄安' is, but it could be a historical or mythological figure.
  • 孟岐 - Similarly, '孟岐' is a name, but its significance is unclear without additional information.
  • 三皇 - The 'Three Sovereigns' are mythical rulers in Chinese mythology, often associated with the early stages of Chinese civilization.
  • 伏羲 - '伏羲' is a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often credited with the invention of writing and the creation of the *I Ching* (Book of Changes).
  • 神农 - '神农' is another legendary figure, often associated with agriculture and medicine.
  • 祝融 - '祝融' is a legendary fire deity, often associated with the south and the element of fire.
  • 黄帝 - '黄帝' is the legendary Yellow Emperor, a central figure in Chinese mythology and the founder of the Huaxia people.
  • 颛顼 - '颛顼' is a legendary ruler, often associated with the north and the element of water.
  • 帝喾 - '帝喾' is another legendary ruler, often associated with the west and the element of metal.
  • 尧 - '尧' is the legendary Emperor Yao, a central figure in Chinese mythology known for his virtue and wisdom.
  • 舜 - '舜' is the legendary Emperor Shun, another central figure in Chinese mythology known for his virtue and wisdom.
  • 夏禹 - '夏禹' is the legendary Emperor Yu, known for his efforts in flood control and the establishment of the Xia Dynasty.
  • 殷汤 - '殷汤' is the legendary Emperor Tang of Shang, known for overthrowing the Xia Dynasty and founding the Shang Dynasty.
  • 文王 - '文王' is the legendary King Wen of Zhou, known for his wisdom and the development of the *I Ching*.

Proper Nouns

  • 总纂官 - Chief Compiler
  • 陆锡熊 - Lu Xixiong
  • 孙士毅 - Sun Shiyi
  • 总校官 - Chief Proofreader
  • 陆费墀 - Lu Feichi
  • 钦定四库全书 - Imperial Decree Four Treasuries
  • 神仙一 - Immortals Volume One

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 守藏史 - A title referring to a historian or keeper of records, often associated with Laozi in Daoist tradition.
  • 未可据也 - Means 'cannot be relied upon' or 'is not credible.'
  • 伏羲以来 - Means 'since the time of Fuxi,' referring to the legendary first ruler of China.
  • 其实论之 - Means 'in fact, when discussing it.'
  • 得道之尤精者 - Means 'one who has attained the Dao with particular excellence.'
  • 按史记 - Means 'according to the Records of the Grand Historian.'
  • 之子 - Means 'his son.'
  • 有功 - Means 'had merit or achieved success.'
  • 仕于汉 - Means 'served the Han dynasty.'
  • 本神灵耳 - Means 'was originally a divine being.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 范蠡 - A historical figure known for his role in the state of Yue and his later life as a merchant.
  • 鸱夷子 - Another name for Fan Li, possibly referring to his life as a merchant.
  • 陶朱公 - A title given to Fan Li after his success as a merchant.
  • 羣书 - Refers to a variety of books or texts.
  • 神仙正经 - Canonical texts on immortals and the Daoist pursuit of immortality.
  • 稚川云 - Ge Hong, a famous Daoist alchemist and writer.
  • 洪以为 - Ge Hong's belief.
  • 天之精神 - The spiritual essence of heaven.
  • 三代 - The Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties.
  • 晚学之徒 - Later scholars or students.
  • 好竒尚异 - Love for the strange and unusual.
  • 茍欲推崇老子 - Desire to exalt Laozi.
  • 其实论之 - In fact, when discussing it.
  • 得道之尤精者 - One who has attained the Dao with particular excellence.
  • 按史记 - According to the Records of the Grand Historian.
  • 之子 - His son.
  • 事魏 - Served the state of Wei.
  • 为将军 - Was a general.
  • 有功 - Had merit or achieved success.
  • 封于段 - Was enfeoffed in the region of Duan.
  • 仕于汉 - Served the Han dynasty.
  • 解为胶西王太傅 - Xie served as the Grand Tutor to the King of Jiaoxi.
  • 家于齐 - Settled in the state of Qi.
  • 本神灵耳 - Was originally a divine being.
  • 浅见道士 - Superficial or unenlightened Daoists.
  • 欲以老子为神异 - Wanted to make Laozi into a divine and extraordinary figure.
  • 使后代学 - To make later generations learn.

Proper Nouns

  • 守藏史 - A title referring to a historian or keeper of records.
  • 范蠡 - A historical figure known for his role in the state of Yue and his later life as a merchant.
  • 鸱夷子 - Another name for Fan Li, possibly referring to his life as a merchant.
  • 陶朱公 - A title given to Fan Li after his success as a merchant.
  • 羣书 - A variety of books or texts.
  • 神仙正经 - Canonical texts on immortals and the Daoist pursuit of immortality.
  • 稚川 - Ge Hong, a famous Daoist alchemist and writer.
  • 洪 - Ge Hong.
  • 天之精神 - The spiritual essence of heaven.
  • 伏羲 - The legendary first ruler of China.
  • 三代 - The Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties.
  • 晚学之徒 - Later scholars or students.
  • 好竒尚异 - Love for the strange and unusual.
  • 茍欲推崇老子 - Desire to exalt Laozi.
  • 其实论之 - In fact, when discussing it.
  • 得道之尤精者 - One who has attained the Dao with particular excellence.
  • 按史记 - According to the Records of the Grand Historian.
  • 之子 - His son.
  • 有功 - Had merit or achieved success.
  • 仕于汉 - Served the Han dynasty.
  • 本神灵耳 - Was originally a divine being.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 者从之而不知此更使不信长生之可学也 - If one follows someone without understanding the deeper meaning, it leads to disbelief in the possibility of learning immortality.
  • 若谓老子是得道者则人必勉力竞慕 - If one says that Laozi is a person who has attained the Dao, then people will surely strive and compete to emulate him.
  • 若谓是神异则非可学也 - If one says that he is a divine being or something extraordinary, then it is not something that can be learned.
  • 或云老子欲西度关关令尹喜知其非常人也从之问道老子惊怪故吐舌聃然遂有老聃之号亦不然也 - Some say Laozi wanted to cross the western pass, and the magistrate of the pass, Yin Xi, knew that he was not an ordinary person and followed him to ask questions. Laozi was surprised and amazed, so he stuck out his tongue, which became the reason for the name 'Lao Dan.' However, this is not necessarily true.
  • 今按九变及元生十二化经老子未入关时固已名聃矣老子数易名字非但一聃而已所以尔者按九宫及三五经及元辰经云人生各有厄防到其时若易名字以随元气之变则可以延年度厄今世有道者亦多如此老子在周乃三百余年二百年之中必有厄防非一是以名稍多耳欲正定老子本末故当以史书实録为主并考仙经秘文以相叅审其他若俗説多 - According to the *Nine Transformations* and *Twelve Metamorphoses* texts, Laozi had already been named 'Dān' before entering the pass, and he changed his name multiple times, not just once. The reason for this is explained by the *Nine Palaces* and *Three and Five Cycles* texts, which suggest that people have different fates and must change their names to follow the changes in the primordial energy to extend their lifespan and overcome obstacles. Many contemporary sages also do this. Laozi lived in the Zhou dynasty for more than three hundred years, and within two hundred years, he must have faced many obstacles, hence the multiple name changes. To accurately determine the origin and end of Laozi, one should primarily rely on historical records and also examine esoteric texts to verify other popular sayings.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 老子 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism.
  • 尹喜 - The magistrate of the western pass who is said to have encountered Laozi.
  • 九变及元生十二化经 - Esoteric Daoist texts that discuss transformation and metamorphosis.
  • 九宫及三五经及元辰经 - Other Daoist texts that discuss the nine palaces, the three and five cycles, and the annual celestial texts, which are part of Daoist cosmology and fate.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 者从之而不知此更使不信长生之可学也 - This phrase is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the relationship between following someone and disbelief in the possibility of learning immortality.
  • 或云老子欲西度关关令尹喜知其非常人也从之问道老子惊怪故吐舌聃然遂有老聃之号亦不然也 - This sentence is complex and requires breaking down into smaller, more manageable parts to understand the sequence of events and the reasons behind them.
  • 今按九变及元生十二化经老子未入关时固已名聃矣老子数易名字非但一聃而已所以尔者按九宫及三五经及元辰经云人生各有厄防到其时若易名字以随元气之变则可以延年度厄今世有道者亦多如此老子在周乃三百余年二百年之中必有厄防非一是以名稍多耳欲正定老子本末故当以史书实録为主并考仙经秘文以相叅审其他若俗説多 - This is a very long and complex sentence that requires careful parsing to understand the logical flow and the reasons given for Laozi's multiple name changes.

Proper Nouns

  • 老子 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism.
  • 尹喜 - The magistrate of the western pass.
  • 九变及元生十二化经 - Esoteric Daoist texts.
  • 九宫及三五经及元辰经 - Other Daoist texts.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 虗妄洪按 - Title or heading, possibly indicating the author or compiler of the text.
  • 西升中胎及复命苞及珠韬玉机金篇内经 - List of texts attributed to Laozi, including *Xisheng Zhongtai* (The Inner Chapters of the Westward Ascension), *Fuming Bao* (The Secret of Rebirth), *Zhutao Yujī* (The Jade Mechanism of the Pearl), and *Jinpiannèijing* (The Inner Canon of the Golden Chapter).
  • 黄白色美眉广颡长耳大目疎齿方口厚唇额有三五达理日角月悬鼻纯骨防柱耳有三漏门足蹈二五手把十文 - Detailed physical description of Laozi, including his appearance and physical features.
  • 周文王时为守藏史至武王时为柱下史 - Historical references to Laozi's roles during the reigns of King Wen and King Wu of Zhou.
  • 时俗见其乆夀故号之为老子 - Indicates that Laozi was given his name due to his long life.
  • 夫人受命自有通神逺见者禀气与常人不同应为道主故能为天神所济众仙所从 - Describes Laozi's spiritual qualities and his role as a spiritual leader.
  • 所出度世之法九丹八石金醴金液次存素守一思神歴藏行气錬形消灾辟恶治飬性絶谷变化厌胜教戒役使魅之法凡九百三十卷符书七十卷 - Lists various methods and texts attributed to Laozi, including alchemical practices, spiritual exercises, and magical rituals.
  • 皆老子本起中篇所记者也自有目録其不在此数者皆后之道士私所増益非真文也 - Indicates that these texts are part of a larger collection and that some may be later additions.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 周文王 - King Wen of Zhou, a legendary ruler of the Zhou dynasty.
  • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou, the son of King Wen and a key figure in the Zhou conquest of the Shang dynasty.
  • 老子 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism and the author of the *Daodejing*.
  • 九丹八石金醴金液 - Refers to alchemical practices and substances used in Daoist immortality techniques.
  • 素守一思神歴藏行气錬形消灾辟恶治飬性絶谷变化厌胜教戒役使魅之法 - Various Daoist practices and techniques, including meditation, alchemy, and magical rituals.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sentence providing a detailed description of Laozi and his roles during the Zhou dynasty needs to be broken down into more manageable segments in English.
  • The sentence describing Laozi's spiritual qualities and his role as a spiritual leader needs to be parsed to clarify the relationships and attributes mentioned.
  • The sentence listing various methods and texts attributed to Laozi needs to be broken down to ensure each method and text is clearly identified.

Proper Nouns

  • 虗妄洪 - Likely the name of the author or compiler of the text.
  • 西升中胎 - *Xisheng Zhongtai* (The Inner Chapters of the Westward Ascension).
  • 复命苞 - *Fuming Bao* (The Secret of Rebirth).
  • 珠韬玉机金篇内经 - *Zhutao Yujī Jinpiannèijing* (The Jade Mechanism of the Pearl and the Inner Canon of the Golden Chapter).
  • 周文王 - King Wen of Zhou.
  • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou.
  • 老子 - Laozi.
  • 九丹八石金醴金液 - Alchemical substances and practices.
  • 素守一思神歴藏行气錬形消灾辟恶治飬性絶谷变化厌胜教戒役使魅之法 - Various Daoist practices and techniques.
  • 九百三十卷符书七十卷 - Numbers of texts and volumes attributed to Laozi.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 和光同尘 - A metaphorical expression meaning to blend in with the world without standing out, often used to describe a person who lives in harmony with nature and society.
  • 内实自然 - Means 'internally, it is natural,' suggesting a state of being that aligns with the natural order.
  • 道成乃去 - Means 'once the Dao is achieved, one departs,' indicating that the person leaves the world after attaining spiritual perfection.
  • 骄气与多欲淫志 - Refers to arrogance, excessive desires, and wild ambitions, which are seen as detrimental to one's spiritual development.
  • 夫鹄不日浴而白 - A metaphor comparing the inherent purity of a swan to the natural state of a person who follows the Dao.
  • 地之自厚矣 - Means 'Earth naturally becomes thick,' symbolizing the inherent stability and depth of the earth.
  • 日月自明矣 - Means 'the sun and moon naturally shine brightly,' symbolizing the inherent clarity and illumination of the cosmos.
  • 星辰固自列矣 - Means 'stars and constellations naturally align themselves,' symbolizing the inherent order and harmony of the celestial bodies.
  • 草木固有区矣 - Means 'plants and trees naturally have their own places,' symbolizing the inherent diversity and organization of nature.
  • 夫子修道 - Means 'Master, you cultivate the Dao,' referring to Confucius' pursuit of the Dao.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 和光同尘 - A Daoist concept emphasizing the importance of blending in with the world without standing out.
  • 内实自然 - A Daoist idea that true virtue is natural and spontaneous.
  • 道成乃去 - A Daoist belief that once one achieves the Dao, they leave the world to join the immortals.
  • 仁义 - A central concept in Confucianism, referring to benevolence and righteousness.
  • 夫鹄不日浴而白 - A metaphor comparing the inherent purity of a swan to the natural state of a person who follows the Dao.
  • 地之自厚矣 - A metaphor for the inherent stability and depth of the earth.
  • 日月自明矣 - A metaphor for the inherent clarity and illumination of the cosmos.
  • 星辰固自列矣 - A metaphor for the inherent order and harmony of the celestial bodies.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 而趋则以至矣 - Contrast with the following rhetorical question, indicating that rushing does not necessarily lead to success.
  • 若击皷以求亡羊乎 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'like striking drums to find a lost sheep,' criticizing the futility of using force or excessive effort to achieve a goal.
  • 夫子乃乱人之性也 - Criticism of imposing external rules or methods on human nature.
  • 使道可献人则人莫不献之其君 - Conditional statement emphasizing the universal appeal of the Way.
  • 使道可告人则人莫不告之兄弟矣 - Conditional statement extending the idea to familial relationships.
  • 使道可传人则人莫不传之其子矣 - Conditional statement emphasizing the generational transmission of the Way.
  • 然而不可者无他也 - Explanation that the reason the Way cannot be imposed is not because of anything else.
  • 中无主而道不可居也 - Explanation that the Way cannot reside in people because there is no inner master.
  • 丘治诗书礼乐易春秋 - Confucius's study of the Six Classics: *Poetry*, *Book of Documents*, *Rites*, *Music*, *Changes*, and *Spring and Autumn Annals*.
  • 诵先王之道明周召之迹 - Reciting the ways of the ancient kings and clarifying the deeds of Duke Zhou and Duke Shao.
  • 以干七十余君而不见用 - Confucius's efforts to seek service from more than seventy rulers but not being employed.
  • 甚矣人之难说也 - Emphasis on the difficulty of persuading people.
  • 夫六艺先王之陈迹也 - The Six Arts are the traces left by the ancient kings.
  • 岂其所陈哉 - Questioning the value of these records.
  • 今子所修者皆因陈迹也 - Criticism of relying on historical texts.
  • 迹者履之出而迹岂异哉 - Questioning the distinction between the traces and the original actions.
  • 孔子归三日不谈 - Confucius returned home and did not speak for three days, indicating deep reflection.
  • 子贡怪而问之 - Zigong found this strange and asked him, showing curiosity and concern.
  • 吾饰意 - Confucius's effort to refine his intentions.

  • 王造昆仑时饮蜂山石髓 - Means 'the king visited Kunlun Mountain and drank the essence of stones from Bee Mountain.' '石髓' refers to the essence or marrow of stones, often associated with immortality or longevity.

  • 皆得飞冲天之道 - Means 'all attained the way of flying and ascending to the heavens.' '飞冲天' refers to the ability to fly or ascend to heaven, often associated with immortality.

  • 张云锦之帏 - Means 'spread the cloud brocade curtains.' '云锦' refers to a type of brocade.
  • 燃九光之灯 - Means 'lit the lamps of nine lights.' '九光之灯' refers to lamps with nine lights.
  • 列玉门之 - Means 'arranged the jade gate's wine.' '玉门' refers to a jade gate, and '' refers to wine.
  • 酌蒲萄之醴 - Means 'poured the grape liquor.' '蒲萄之醴' refers to grape wine.
  • 宫监香果为天宫之 - Means 'the palace attendants and fragrant fruits were arranged as offerings for the Heavenly Palace.' '天宫之' refers to offerings for the Heavenly Palace.
  • 帝乃盛服立于阶下 - Means 'the emperor then dressed in his finest attire and stood at the foot of the stairs.' '盛服' means finest attire.

  • 须臾转近闻云中箫鼓之声人马之响 - Means 'soon it turned closer, and the sound of flutes and drums and the noise of people and horses were heard from the cloud.' '箫鼓之声' refers to the sound of flutes and drums, and '人马之响' refers to the noise of people and horses.
  • 半食顷王母至也 - Means 'after half a meal's time, the Queen Mother arrived.' '半食顷' means half a meal's time, which is about 30 minutes.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 言都阿母昔出配北烛仙人近又召还使领命禄真灵官也 - This sentence is a complex statement about the mother of Xuan Du being sent to marry the Northern Candle Immortal and then recalled to be appointed as the true immortal official in charge of destiny and fate.
  • 帝于是登延灵之台盛斋存道其四方之事权委于冡宰焉 - This sentence describes the emperor ascending the platform of extending immortality, holding a grand ceremony to preserve the Dao, and entrusting the affairs of the four directions to the Grand Steward.
  • 到七月七日乃修除宫掖设坐大殿以紫罗荐地燔百和之香张云锦之帏燃九光之灯列玉门之酌蒲萄之醴宫监香果为天宫之 - This long sentence describes the preparations made on the seventh day of the seventh month, including cleaning the palace, setting up a seat, spreading purple silk, burning fragrant herbs, spreading cloud brocade curtains, lighting lamps with nine lights, arranging the jade gate's wine, pouring grape liquor, and arranging offerings for the Heavenly Palace.

  • 又命侍女更索桃果 - Means 'then ordered the maids to fetch more peach fruits,' indicating a request for more food.
  • 须臾以玉盘盛仙桃七颗 - Means 'in a moment, they brought seven divine peaches on a jade plate,' indicating the speed and elegance of the service.
  • 大如鸭卵形圆青色 - Means 'as large as duck eggs, round and green in color,' describing the appearance of the peaches.
  • 以呈王母母以四颗与帝三颗自食 - Means 'to present to the Queen Mother, who gave four to the emperor and ate three herself,' indicating a gesture of generosity and equality.
  • 桃味甘美口有盈味 - Means 'the peach tasted sweet and left a lingering flavor,' emphasizing its deliciousness.
  • 王母问帝帝曰欲种之母曰此桃三千年一生实中夏地 - Means 'the Queen Mother asked the emperor, who said he wanted to plant them. The Queen Mother replied that these peaches take three thousand years to bear fruit in the Central Plains,' indicating the extraordinary nature of the peaches.

  • 歌毕王母曰 - Literal translation: 'after the song ended, the Queen Mother said'; indicates the conclusion of the musical performance and the beginning of a speech.
  • 夫欲修身当营其气 - Literal translation: 'those who wish to cultivate themselves should cultivate their qi (vital energy)'; introduces a philosophical concept.
  • 太仙真经所谓行益易之道 - Literal translation: 'as the True Scripture of the Great Immortals says, the path of increasing ease'; refers to a religious text.
  • 益者益精易者易形 - Literal translation: 'those who increase will increase their essence, and those who make things easy will make their form easy'; a metaphorical statement about spiritual and physical transformation.
  • 能益能易名上仙籍 - Literal translation: 'those who can increase and make things easy will be recorded in the register of the highest immortals'; indicates a reward for spiritual achievement.
  • 不益不易不离死厄 - Literal translation: 'those who do not increase and do not make things easy will not escape the fate of death'; emphasizes the importance of spiritual cultivation.
  • 行益易者谓常思灵宝也 - Literal translation: 'those who practice the path of increasing ease should always think of the spiritual treasure'; a metaphorical statement about the importance of spiritual focus.
  • 灵者神也寳者精也 - Literal translation: 'the spiritual is the divine, and the treasure is the essence'; explains the metaphors used in the previous statement.
  • 子但爱精握固闭气吞液 - Literal translation: 'you should love your essence, hold it firmly, close your breath, and swallow the fluid'; a set of spiritual practices.
  • 气化为血血化为精 - Literal translation: 'qi transforms into blood, and blood transforms into essence'; a description of the transformation process in spiritual cultivation.
  • 精化为神神化为液 - Literal translation: 'essence transforms into the divine, and the divine transforms into fluid'; continuing the transformation process.
  • 液化为骨行之不倦 - Literal translation: 'fluid transforms into bone, and if you do this without tiring'; emphasizing the importance of persistence in spiritual practice.
  • 神精充溢为之一年 - Literal translation: 'the divine and essence will be abundant, and this will be one year'; indicating a period of spiritual growth.
  • 易气二年易血三年 - Literal translation: 'transform qi in two years, transform blood in three years'; outlining a timeline for spiritual transformation.
  • 易发九年易形形易则变化 - Literal translation: 'transform hair in nine years, transform form, and when the form changes, there will be transformation'; indicating the final stages of spiritual transformation.
  • 变化则道成道成 - Literal translation: 'when there is transformation, the Dao is achieved, and the Dao is achieved'; emphasizing the ultimate goal of spiritual practice.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 則為仙人吐納六氣 - Refers to the practice of breathing in and out the six qi (氣), which are fundamental energies in Chinese cosmology. '吐納' means 'breathing in and out,' and '六氣' refers to the six fundamental energies.
  • 口中甘香欲食芝存得其味 - '口中甘香' means 'sweet and fragrant in the mouth,' and '欲食芝' means 'desiring to eat the lingzhi mushroom.' '存得其味' means 'to retain its flavor.'
  • 防息揖吞从心所适 - '防息' means 'to control one's breath,' '揖吞' means 'to bow and swallow,' and '从心所适' means 'to follow one's heart's desire.'
  • 此元始天王在丹房之中所説防言 - '元始天王' refers to the Primordial Heavenly Emperor, a deity in Taoist cosmology. '丹房' means 'elixir room,' and '所説防言' means 'the words spoken.'
  • 今敇侍笈玉女李庆孙书録之以相付子善録而修焉 - '敇' means 'to command,' '侍笈玉女' refers to a jade maiden who serves as an attendant, and '书録之以相付子善録而修焉' means 'to write and record it to hand it over to you, so you may record and cultivate it well.'
  • 王母语言既毕啸命官使驾龙严车欲去 - '王母' refers to the Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Chinese mythology. '语言既毕' means 'having finished speaking,' '啸命' means 'to summon with a whistle,' '官' refers to officials, and '驾龙严车' means 'to ride on a dragon-drawn chariot.'
  • 帝下席叩头请留殷勤王母乃止 - '帝下席' means 'the emperor got off his seat,' '叩头' means 'to bow his head,' and '殷勤' means 'sincerely.'
  • 王母乃遣侍女郭密香与上元夫人相问云 - '遣' means 'to send,' '侍女' refers to a maid, '郭密香' is the name of the maid, and '相问' means 'to ask.'
  • 王九光之母敬谢但不相见四千余年矣 - '王九光' is a name, '母' means 'mother,' '敬谢' means 'to respectfully thank,' and '四千余年矣' means 'for over four thousand years.'
  • 天事劳我致以愆面汉主好道适来视之见某了了似可成进然形慢神秽脑血淫漏五脏不淳关胄彭孛骨无精液脉浮反升肉多精少瞳子不夷三尸狡乱白失时虽当语之以至道殆恐非仙才也 - This is a complex sentence describing the condition of the person being observed. '天事劳我致以愆面' means 'the heavenly affairs have tired me, causing me to look guilty,' '汉主好道' means 'the Han ruler is fond of the Dao,' '见某了了似可成进' means 'I see that he seems to be able to advance,' '形慢神秽' means 'his form is sluggish and his spirit is impure,' '脑血淫漏' means 'his brain and blood are leaking,' '五脏不淳' means 'his five organs are not pure,' '关胄彭孛' means 'his bones are swollen,' '骨无精液' means 'his bones lack essence and fluid,' '脉浮反升' means 'his pulse is floating and rising,' '肉多精少' means 'his flesh is abundant but his essence is scarce,' '瞳子不夷' means 'his pupils are not level,' '三尸狡乱' means 'the three ghosts are cunning and chaotic,' '白失时' means 'the black and white are out of time,' '虽当语之以至道殆恐非仙才也' means 'even if I were to tell him the highest Dao, I fear he is not worthy of becoming a sage.'
  • 吾乆在人间实为臭浊然时复可游望以写细念庸主对坐悒悒不乐夫人可暂来 - '吾乆在人间' means 'I have been in the human world for a long time,' '实为臭浊' means 'it is indeed foul and turbid,' '时复可游望' means 'I can occasionally wander and look around,' '以写细念' means 'to express my fine thoughts,' '庸主对坐悒悒不乐' means 'the ordinary ruler sitting opposite me is unhappy,' '夫人可暂来' means 'you can come for a short while.'

  • 夫人年可二十余天姿精耀灵眸绝朗服青霜之袍云彩乱色非锦非绣不可名字头作三角髻余发散垂至腰戴九云夜光之冠曳六出火玉之 - Means 'the lady was about twenty years old, with a refined and radiant appearance, her eyes exceptionally bright, wearing a robe of frosty blue, the clouds swirling in colors that are neither silk nor embroidery, impossible to name, with a triangular bun on her head and long hair cascading down to her waist, wearing a nine-cloud night-glow crown and a six-petal fire jade robe.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 且贵上帝之观矣 - Formal expression meaning 'Moreover, the profound view of the Supreme Deity is esteemed.'
  • 子自非受命合神弗见此文矣 - Means 'You would not see this text unless you were divinely chosen and united with the gods.'
  • 今虽得其真形观其妙理 - Means 'Now, even if you obtain its true form and observe its subtle principles.'
  • 而无五帝六甲左右飞之符 - Means 'but without the talismans of the Five Emperors, Six Jia, and the left and right flying spirits.'
  • 太隂六丁通真逐玉女之箓 - Refers to the talisman of the six Ding stars of the Great Yin, which connects to the true and pursues the jade maidens.
  • 太阳六戊招神天光策精之书 - Refers to the book of the six Wu stars of the Sun, which summons divine light and controls essence.
  • 左乙混沌东之文 - Refers to the text of the left Yi, which is chaotic and eastern.
  • 右庚素収摄杀之律 - Refers to the law of the right Geng, which is pure and controls killing.
  • 壬癸六遯地八术 - Refers to the eight techniques of the six遁 (dun) of Ren and Gui, which are hidden and earthly.
  • 丙丁入火赤班符 - Refers to the red banner of fire entering the Bing and Ding.
  • 六辛入金致黄水月华之法 - Refers to the method of the six Xin entering metal to produce yellow water and lunar light.
  • 六巳石精金光藏景化形之方 - Refers to the method of the six Si, which is the essence of stone, golden light, and transformation into form.
  • 子午卯酉八禀十诀 - Refers to the eight endowments and ten rules of the Zi, Wu, Mao, and You.
  • 六咸仪丑辰未戌地真素诀 - Refers to the pure and true rules of the six Yi, which are the earth's true and pure rules.
  • 长生紫书三五顺行 - Refers to the purple book of longevity, which follows the three and five in sequence.

  • 凡此十二事者 - Means 'All of these twelve matters.'
  • 当何以召山朝地神摄总万精驱策百束虎豹役蛟龙乎 - Means 'How can one summon the mountain spirits, face the earth gods, control all spirits, drive the hundred ghosts, bind tigers and leopards, and command dragons and snakes?'
  • 子所谓适知其一未见其他也 - Means 'You only know one aspect and have not seen the others.'

  • 别敇三官司直推夫人之轻泄也 - 'I will issue a separate edict to the Three Offices to investigate Lady's light-hearted leakage.' The phrase '轻泄' means 'light-hearted leakage' or 'casual disclosure.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 月支国王 - ‘月支’ is a historical kingdom, and ‘国王’ means ‘king.’ Together, it refers to the king of the Yuezhi kingdom.
  • 献香 - ‘献’ means ‘to present’ or ‘to offer,’ and ‘香’ means ‘incense.’ Together, it means ‘presented incense.’
  • 猛兽 - ‘猛’ means ‘fierce’ or ‘wild,’ and ‘兽’ means ‘beast.’ Together, it means ‘fierce beast.’
  • 形如五六十日犬子 - ‘形如’ means ‘shaped like,’ ‘五六十日’ means ‘about fifty to sixty days old,’ and ‘犬子’ means ‘puppy.’ Together, it means ‘shaped like a puppy about fifty to sixty days old.’
  • 其毛黄色 - ‘其毛’ means ‘its fur,’ and ‘黄色’ means ‘yellow.’ Together, it means ‘its fur is yellow.’
  • 气秃悴 - ‘气’ means ‘appearance’ or ‘look,’ ‘秃’ means ‘bald’ or ‘withered,’ and ‘悴’ means ‘wasted’ or ‘dejected.’ Together, it means ‘withered and dejected appearance.’
  • 夫威加于百禽者不必计其大小 - ‘夫’ is an introductory particle, ‘威加于’ means ‘exerts authority over,’ ‘百禽’ means ‘many birds,’ ‘不必’ means ‘does not necessarily,’ and ‘计其大小’ means ‘consider its size.’ Together, it means ‘One who exerts authority over many birds does not necessarily consider its size.’
  • 神驎为巨象之王 - ‘神驎’ means ‘divine unicorn,’ ‘为’ means ‘is,’ ‘巨象’ means ‘giant elephant,’ and ‘之王’ means ‘the king.’ Together, it means ‘the divine unicorn is the king of giant elephants.’
  • 凤凰为大之宗 - ‘凤凰’ means ‘phoenix,’ ‘为’ means ‘is,’ ‘大’ means ‘great bird,’ and ‘之宗’ means ‘the leader.’ Together, it means ‘the phoenix is the leader of great birds.’
  • 仰中土而慕道风 - ‘仰’ means ‘look up to,’ ‘中土’ means ‘central lands’ (China), ‘而’ is a conjunction, ‘慕’ means ‘admire,’ and ‘道风’ means ‘way of the Dao.’ Together, it means ‘look up to the central lands and admire the way of the Dao.’
  • 薄金玉而厚灵物 - ‘薄’ means ‘light on,’ ‘金玉’ means ‘gold and jade,’ ‘而’ is a conjunction, ‘厚’ means ‘value highly,’ and ‘灵物’ means ‘spiritual objects.’ Together, it means ‘light on gold and jade but value highly spiritual objects.’
  • 搜竒蕴而索神香 - ‘搜’ means ‘search for,’ ‘竒蕴’ means ‘rare treasures,’ ‘而’ is a conjunction, ‘索’ means ‘seek,’ and ‘神香’ means ‘divine incense.’ Together, it means ‘search for rare treasures and seek divine incense.’

  • 臣国占风之谬也 - Means 'it is a mistake in my country's divination of the wind,' suggesting a misinterpretation or error.

  • 身多动则注贼 - Means 'having many actions leads to attracting thieves,' suggesting that excessive activity can invite trouble.
  • 心多节则奢侈 - Means 'having many desires leads to extravagance,' implying that excessive desires result in wastefulness.
  • 未有用此四多而天下成治者也 - Means 'there has never been a case where using these four excesses has led to the governance of the world,' criticizing the emperor's behavior.
  • 帝黙然不平 - Means 'the emperor was silent but discontented,' indicating a reaction to the criticism.
  • 帝乃使使者勑猛兽发声试听之 - Means 'the emperor then sent an envoy to command the fierce beast to make a sound for testing,' suggesting a test of the beast's power.

  • 帝登时颠蹷掩耳振动不能自止 - Means 'the emperor immediately fell to the ground, covering his ears, and could not stop shaking,' describing the emperor's reaction to the sound.
  • 侍者乃武士皆失仗 - Means 'the attendants, who were all warriors, lost their weapons,' indicating the impact of the sound on the attendants.
  • 帝忌之因以此 - Means 'the emperor feared it and therefore acted accordingly,' suggesting that the emperor took action due to his fear.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 墨子者名翟宋人也 - Standard biographical formula meaning 'Mòzi was a man named Zhí from the state of Song.'
  • 仕宋為大夫 - 'Served as a high official in the state of Song.'
  • 著書十篇號為墨子 - 'Wrote ten volumes of writings, which were called *Mòzi*.' This is the origin of the text *Mòzi*.
  • 世多學者與儒家分途 - 'In the world, many scholars diverged from the Confucian path.' This indicates the emergence of the Mohist school.
  • 務尚儉約 - 'Emphasized frugality and simplicity.'
  • 頗毀孔子 - 'He criticized Confucius to some extent.'
  • 為楚造雲梯之械以攻宋 - 'Built cloud ladders for the state of Chu to attack the state of Song.'
  • 往詣楚 - 'Traveled to the state of Chu.'
  • 腳壊裂裳裹足 - 'His feet were broken; he tore his clothes to wrap his feet.' This is a description of Mòzi's journey, showing his dedication.
  • 七日七夜到見公輸般而説之 - 'After seven days and nights, he arrived and convinced Gōngshū Bān.'
  • 子為雲梯以攻宋宋何罪之有 - 'You are building the cloud ladder to attack Song. What crime has Song committed?'
  • 余於地而不足於民 - 'Song has more land than it needs but not enough people.'
  • 殺所不足而爭所有余 - 'Killing what you lack to fight for what you have in excess.'
  • 不可謂智 - 'This cannot be called wise.'

  • 不可謂忠 - 'This cannot be called loyal.'
  • 不可謂強 - 'This cannot be called strong.'
  • 吾不可以巳言於王矣 - 'I cannot stop. I will go speak to the King.'
  • 舍其文軒隣有一弊舆而欲竊之 - 'If a man abandons his fine chariot and wants to steal a neighbor’s broken cart...'
  • 舍其锦绣隣有短褐而欲竊之 - 'Abandons his fine silk and desires to steal a neighbor’s coarse robe...'
  • 舍其粱肉隣有糟糠而欲竊之 - 'Abandons his fine food and desires to steal his neighbor’s coarse porridge...'
  • 此为何若人也 - 'What kind of person is this?'

  • 公輸般 - Also known as *Gongshu Ban*, a famous master craftsman and inventor from the state of Chu. He is best known for designing the cloud ladder for attacking Song.
  • 云梯之械 - A military device, the 'cloud ladder,' used for siege warfare. It was a type of siege engine.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '可食坐致行厨饭膳供数百人' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can provide food for hundreds of people by sitting and summoning a traveling kitchen' or 'can summon a traveling kitchen to provide food for hundreds of people.'
  • The phrase '又能吹气为风飞砂扬石' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can blow air to create wind and make sand and stones fly' or 'can create wind and make sand and stones fly with a breath.'
  • The phrase '以手指屋宇山陵壶器便欲頽壊' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'with a finger, can cause buildings, mountains, and vessels to collapse' or 'can cause buildings, mountains, and vessels to collapse with a finger.'
  • The phrase '复指之即还如故' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'by pointing again, can restore things to their original state' or 'can restore things to their original state by pointing again.'
  • The phrase '又能化生美女之形' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can transform into the form of a beautiful woman' or 'can create a beautiful woman from nothing.'
  • The phrase '及作水火' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can create water and fire' or 'can manipulate water and fire.'
  • The phrase '又能一日之中行数千里' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can travel thousands of miles in a single day' or 'can cover thousands of miles in a single day.'
  • The phrase '能水兴云奋手起雾' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can summon water, clouds, and fog with a gesture' or 'can create water, clouds, and fog with a gesture.'
  • The phrase '聚土成山刺地成渊' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can create mountains and deep pits with the movement of the earth' or 'can create mountains and deep pits by manipulating the earth.'
  • The phrase '能忽老忽少乍大乍小' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can change age and size at will' or 'can change age and size suddenly.'
  • The phrase '入水不沾歩行水上' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can walk on water without getting wet' or 'can walk on water and not get wet.'
  • The phrase '召江海中鱼鼈蛟龙鼋鼍即皆登岸' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can call aquatic creatures onto the shore' or 'can summon aquatic creatures to the shore.'
  • The phrase '口吐五色之气方广十里直上连天' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can exhale a five-colored gas that spreads for ten miles and reaches the sky' or 'can create a five-colored gas that spreads for ten miles and reaches the sky.'
  • The phrase '又能跃上下去地数百丈' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can leap hundreds of feet into the air and fall back down' or 'can jump hundreds of feet into the air and fall back down.'
  • The phrase '后去不知所在' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'disappears and is no longer known to be in a particular place' or 'disappears and is no longer seen.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 玉子者姓章名震南郡人也 - Classical biographical formula meaning 'Yuzi, surnamed Zhang, named Zhen, from the state of Nanyang.'
  • 少好学 - Means 'young and fond of learning,' indicating early intellectual inclination.
  • 众经周幽王征之不出 - '众经周' likely refers to the Zhou Dynasty, and '幽王' is the posthumous name of King You of Zhou. '征之不出' means 'he was summoned but did not appear.'
  • 人生世间日失一日 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'each day spent in the world is a day lost.'
  • 贪富贵不知飬性命 - '贪富贵' means 'greedily pursuing wealth and status,' and '不知飬性命' means 'unaware of nurturing one's life.'
  • 命尽气绝则死 - Means 'when life ends and breath ceases, death follows.'
  • 位为王侯金玉如山何益于灰土乎 - '位为王侯' means 'holding the position of king or marquis,' and '金玉如山' means 'mountains of gold and jade.' The phrase '何益于灰土乎' is a rhetorical question meaning 'what benefit do they bring to the dust?'
  • 独有神仙度世可以无穷耳 - '独有神仙度世' means 'only immortals can transcend the world,' and '可以无穷耳' means 'and can thus be eternal.'
  • 乃师长桑子具受众术 - '师长桑子' means 'he became a disciple of Changsang Zi,' and '具受众术' means 'and learned all his techniques.'
  • 乃别造一家之法 - Means 'and then created his own unique system.'
  • 着道书百余篇 - Means 'wrote over a hundred books on Daoism.'
  • 其术以务魁为主 - '务魁' is likely a specific technique or practice, possibly related to alchemy or spiritual cultivation.
  • 精于五行之意 - Means 'he was skilled in the principles of the Five Elements.'
  • 演其微妙以飬性治病消灾散祸 - '演其微妙' means 'expounded its subtleties,' and '以飬性治病消灾散祸' means 'to cultivate the self, heal diseases, eliminate disasters, and disperse calamities.'
  • 能起飘风发屋折木作雷雨云雾 - Means 'he could summon fierce winds, lift roofs, break trees, and create thunder, rain, clouds, and mists.'
  • 能以木瓦石为六畜龙虎立成 - Means 'he could transform wood, tiles, and stones into cattle, horses, tigers, and dragons instantly.'
  • 能分形为百千人 - Means 'he could divide his form into hundreds or thousands of people.'
  • 能渉江海含水喷之皆成珠玉 - Means 'he could cross rivers and seas, hold water in his mouth, and spit it out as pearls and jade.'
  • 或时闭气不息举之不起推之不动屈之不曲伸之不直 - Means 'sometimes he could hold his breath, and when lifted, he would not rise; when pushed, he would not move; when bent, he would not curve; when stretched, he would not straighten.'
  • 或百日数十日乃起 - Means 'sometimes he would remain like this for hundreds or tens of days before returning to normal.'
  • 每与弟子行各丸泥为马与之皆令闭目须臾成大马乗之日行千里 - Means 'whenever he traveled with his disciples, he would each roll mud into horses, and when they closed their eyes, the horses would instantly become large ones, allowing them to travel a thousand miles in a day.'
  • 又能吐气五色起数丈见飞鸟过指之即堕 - Means 'he could also exhale five-colored breath that rose several zhang high, and when he saw a flying bird, he could point at it and make it fall.'
  • 临渊投符召鱼鼈之属悉来上岸 - Means 'when he stood by a deep pool and threw down a talisman, all kinds of fish and turtles would come ashore.'
  • 能令弟子举眼见千里外物亦不能乆也 - Means 'he could make his disciples see things a thousand miles away, but they could not do so for long.'
  • 其务魁时以器盛水着两肘之间之水上立有赤光辉辉起一丈以此水治病病在内饮之在外者洗之皆立愈 - '务魁时' refers to a specific time or state of practice. '以器盛水着两肘之间' means 'he would hold water in a vessel between his elbows,' and '之水上立有赤光辉辉起一丈' means 'when he poured it onto the water, a red glow would rise a zhang high.' '以此水治病病在内饮之在外者洗之皆立愈' means 'using this water to treat diseases, those inside would drink it, and those outside would wash with it, and all would be instantly cured.'
  • 后入崆峒山合丹白日升天而去 - '合丹' means 'to concoct elixirs,' and '白日升天而去' means 'ascended to heaven in broad daylight and departed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 玉子 - The name 'Yuzi' (玉子) is likely a pen name or title, possibly referring to a historical figure or a legendary Daoist.
  • 长桑子 - 'Changsang Zi' is a legendary Daoist figure often associated with alchemy and immortality.
  • 周幽王 - King You of Zhou, the last king of the Western Zhou Dynasty, known for his corruption and the eventual fall of the dynasty.
  • 五行 - The Five Elements (Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, Water) are a fundamental concept in Chinese philosophy and Daoism.
  • 崆峒山 - Mount Kungtung, a mythical mountain in Chinese folklore often associated with Daoist immortals and alchemy.

  • 宫殿郁郁如云气五色黄不可名状 - The palace was grand, like clouds with five colors of purple and yellow, indescribable in appearance.
  • 侍者数百人多女少男庭中有珠玉之树众芝丛生龙虎成群游戏其间 - There were hundreds of attendants, mostly women, and in the courtyard, there were trees of jade and precious stones, clusters of various types of mushrooms, and dragons and tigers playing together.
  • 闻琅琅如铜铁之声不知何等 - He heard a sound like metal ringing, but did not know what it was.

  • 须臾数玉女持金案玉杯来赐羲曰此是神丹饮者不死夫妻各一杯夀万歳 - Soon, several jade maidens brought golden trays and jade cups, offering him a potion called Shen Dan, which grants immortality. They gave him and his wife a cup each, promising them a lifespan of ten thousand years.
  • 乃吿言饮服毕拜而勿谢服药后赐二枚大如鸡子脯五寸遗羲曰暂还人间治百姓疾病如欲上来书此符悬之竿杪吾当迎汝 - They instructed him to drink the potion and then bow without thanking them. After taking the medicine, they gave him two pills as large as eggs and five inches thick, telling him to return to the human world temporarily to treat the common people's illnesses. If he wanted to ascend again, he should write this talisman and hang it at the top of a pole, and they would come to meet him.
  • 羲奄忽如寐已在地上多得其符验也 - Xu suddenly fell asleep and woke up on the ground, having received many of these talismans, which proved to be effective.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 承言早往期处到日西不见一人乃起欲去曰书生定欺我耳 - Narrative sequence: 'In response to the words, went early, expecting to arrive at sunset, didn't see anyone, then got up to leave, saying, 'The scholar must have deceived me.'
  • 二人已在其侧呼曰安世汝来何晚也 - Dialogue: 'Two people were already beside him, called out saying, 'An Shi, why did you come so late?'
  • 答曰早来但不见君耳 - Response: 'Replied saying, 'Came early, but didn't see you.'
  • 二人曰吾端坐在汝邉耳频三期之而安世辄早至知可教乃以药二丸与安世诫之曰汝归勿复饮食别止于一处 - Dialogue and instruction: 'The two said, 'We were sitting right beside you, repeatedly waited for three days, but An Shi always came early, knew he could be taught, then gave him two pills, warned him saying, 'You should not eat or drink again, stay in one place separately.'
  • 叔本怪之安世处空室何得有人语往辄不见叔本曰向闻多人语声今不见一人何也答曰我独语耳 - Dialogue and explanation: 'Shu Ben was puzzled, 'How could there be someone speaking in An Shi's empty room, he went and didn't see anyone, Shu Ben said, 'I heard the sound of many people speaking earlier, but now I don't see anyone, why is that? I replied, 'I was just talking to myself.'
  • 叔本见安世不复食但饮水止息别位疑非常人自知失贤乃叹曰夫道尊徳贵不在年齿父母生我然非师则莫能使我长生先闻道者即为师矣乃执弟子之礼朝夕拜事之为之洒扫安世道成白日升天临去遂以要道术授叔本叔本后亦仙去矣 - Narrative sequence: 'Shu Ben saw that An Shi no longer ate, only drank water, and rested in a separate place, suspected he was not an ordinary person, realized he had lost a virtuous person, then sighed and said, 'The way is respected and virtue is precious, not in age, parents give birth to us, but without a teacher, no one can make us live forever, those who first hear the way are our teachers, then treated him with the respect of a disciple, bowing and serving him morning and evening, swept and cleaned for him, An Shi achieved the way and ascended to heaven at noon, before leaving, he taught Shu Ben the essential methods, Shu Ben later also ascended to heaven.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 神仙传 - Refers to the *Shenxian Zhuan* (Records of Immortals), a collection of stories about immortals and their practices.
  • 道尊徳贵 - Refers to the Confucian and Taoist values of respect for the way and virtue.
  • 先闻道者即为师矣 - A philosophical statement emphasizing the importance of those who have first learned the way as teachers.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sequence must be read as a continuous narrative of the protagonist's actions and thoughts.
  • This dialogue must be read as a direct address from the two people to the protagonist.
  • This response must be read as the protagonist's reply to the two people.
  • This dialogue and instruction must be read as the two people explaining their repeated waits and the protagonist's consistent early arrival, followed by the instruction to take the pills.
  • This dialogue and explanation must be read as Shu Ben's puzzlement and the protagonist's self-explanation.
  • This narrative sequence must be read as Shu Ben's realization and the final events, including the protagonist's ascension and the transmission of the essential methods.

Proper Nouns

  • 安世 - The name of the protagonist.
  • 叔本 - The name of the person who becomes a disciple.
  • 神仙传 - The title of the text from which this passage is taken.

  • 着青裙入天门 - A ritualistic or symbolic act, where '着青裙' (wearing a green skirt) and '入天门' (entering the heavenly gate) suggest a spiritual or divine journey.
  • 揖金母拜木公 - A formal act of bowing to '金母' (Jinmu, the Queen Mother of the West) and '木公' (Mugong, the East King Father), indicating reverence to deities.
  • 时人莫知之 - A phrase meaning 'no one at the time knew it,' suggesting a hidden or misunderstood event.
  • 子房知之 - 'Zi Fang' (Zhang Liang) is a historical figure known for his wisdom and involvement in the Han dynasty. '知之' means 'knew it,' indicating his awareness of the event.
  • 此东王公之玉童也 - A statement identifying the child as a '玉童' (jade child) of the East King Father, suggesting a divine or supernatural status.
  • 所谓金母者西王母也木公者东王公也 - A clarification that 'Jinmu' is the Queen Mother of the West and 'Mugong' is the East King Father, emphasizing their roles as deities.
  • 木公为男仙之主金母为女仙之宗 - A statement that Mugong is the master of male immortals and Jinmu is the ancestor of female immortals, indicating their roles in the hierarchy of immortals.
  • 长生飞化之士升天之初先觐金母后谒木公 - A ritualistic sequence where immortals first pay homage to Jinmu and then to Mugong before ascending to heaven, emphasizing the order of reverence.
  • 此歌乃玉童教世人拜王公而揖王母也 - A statement that the song is taught by the jade child to teach people to worship the King Father and bow to the Queen Mother, indicating the song's purpose.
  • 子房佐汉封留侯为大司徒 - A statement that Zhang Liang assisted the Han dynasty and was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Liu, holding the position of Grand Tutor, indicating his historical role.
  • 赤眉之乱人发其墓 - A statement that during the Red Eyebrow Rebellion, someone disturbed his tomb, indicating a historical event.
  • 但见黄石枕化而飞去若流星焉不见其尸形衣冠 - A phrase meaning 'only a yellow stone pillow was seen, which transformed and flew away like a shooting star; his body, clothes, and hat were not seen,' suggesting a miraculous transformation.
  • 其孙道陵得道朝昆仑之夕子房往焉 - A statement that his grandson Tao Ling attained the Dao and, on the evening he visited Mount Kunlun, Zhang Liang went with him, indicating a continuation of his spiritual journey.
  • 出仙传拾遗 - A phrase indicating the source of the text, '拾遗' (Shiyi) meaning 'Miscellaneous Collection,' suggesting it is a compilation of supernatural stories.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 挂布化为龙 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the cloth hung in the wind transformed into a dragon.' Describes a transformation or miracle.
  • 因名其地为布龙泽 - Literally means 'therefore, the place was named 'Bulong Ze'.' The name 'Bulong Ze' is likely a local or regional name with no direct English equivalent.
  • 朔以元封中游鸿蒙之泽 - 'Shuo in the Yuanfeng period traveled through the vast expanse of Hongmeng.' 'Yuanfeng' is a reign title of Emperor Wu of Han, and 'Hongmeng' is a mythical place often associated with the beginning of the universe.
  • 忽遇母采桑于白海之濵 - 'Suddenly encountered his mother picking mulberries by the shore of Baihai.' 'Baihai' is a mythical sea, often associated with the eastern sea in Chinese mythology.
  • 俄而有黄眉翁指母以语朔 - 'Soon, an old man with a yellow beard pointed to his mother and spoke to Shuo.' This is a narrative device to introduce a supernatural or wise figure.
  • 昔为我妻托形为太白之精 - 'Once she was my wife, transformed into the essence of the planet Venus (Tai Bai).' 'Tai Bai' is the Chinese name for Venus, often associated with beauty and divinity.
  • 今汝亦此星之精也 - 'Now you are also the essence of this star.' This suggests a divine or celestial lineage.
  • 吾却食吞气已九千余年 - 'I have abstained from food and inhaled energy for over nine thousand years.' This is a hyperbolic expression of longevity and spiritual practice.
  • 目中童子皆有青光能见幽之物 - 'The pupils in my eyes have a green glow, enabling me to see hidden things.' This describes a supernatural ability.
  • 吾生来已三洗髓五伐毛矣 - 'Since my birth, I have undergone three bone and marrow cleansings and five skin and feather sheddings.' This emphasizes the speaker's long life and numerous transformations.
  • 朔既长仕汉武帝为太中大夫 - 'Shuo, having grown up, served Emperor Wu of Han as a Taizhong Daifu.' 'Taizhong Daifu' is a high-ranking official position.
  • 武帝暮年好仙术与朔狎昵 - 'In his later years, Emperor Wu was fond of Daoist practices and became close to Shuo.' This indicates a personal relationship between the emperor and Shuo.
  • 一日谓朔曰朕欲使爱幸者不老可乎 - 'One day, he said to Shuo, 'Can I make those I love remain young?'' This is a request for immortality or longevity.
  • 朔曰臣能之 - 'Shuo replied, 'I can do it.' This is a straightforward response.
  • 帝曰服何药 - 'The emperor asked, 'What medicine should be taken?'' This is a practical question.
  • 曰东北地有芝草西南有春生之鱼 - 'Shuo replied, 'In the northeast, there is the grass of immortality; in the southwest, there is the spring-born fish.' These are mythical ingredients for longevity.
  • 帝曰何知之 - 'The emperor asked, 'How do you know this?'' This is a request for the source of the information.
  • 曰三足乌欲下地食此草羲和以手掩乌目不许下畏其食此草也 - 'Shuo replied, 'The three-legged crow wanted to descend to eat this grass, but Xihe covered its eyes and forbade it, fearing it would eat the grass.' This is a mythological explanation.
  • 鸟兽食此即美闷不能动 - 'If birds and beasts eat it, they will become paralyzed and unable to move.' This describes the effects of the grass.
  • 问曰子何知之 - 'He asked, 'How do you know this?'' This is a repetition of the emperor's earlier question.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 曰吉云五露可得否 - A direct question asking if 'the five dews' can be obtained, where '吉云' (Jiyun) is a proper noun, possibly a person or a title.
  • 臣负吉云草以备马 - A statement indicating that the speaker (臣, minister) is carrying 'Jiyun grass' to prepare for a horse, where '负' (fu) means 'to carry' or 'to bear'.
  • 此立可得日可三二往乃东走至夕而还得白青黄露盛以青琉璃各受五合授帝帝徧赐羣臣其得之者老者皆少疾者皆除也 - A complex sentence describing a series of actions and outcomes. '此立可得' means 'this can be obtained immediately', '日可三二往' means 'can go three or four times a day', '乃东走至夕而还得' means 'then go east until evening and return', '白青黄露盛' refers to various colored dews, '以青琉璃各受五合' means 'using green glass containers, each holding five units', '授帝帝徧赐羣臣' means 'presented to the emperor, who then distributed it to all officials', and '其得之者老者皆少疾者皆除也' means 'those who received it, the old became young, and the sick were cured'.
  • 又武帝常见彗星朔折指星木以授帝帝指彗星应时星没时人莫之测也 - Another complex sentence. '又武帝常见彗星' means 'Emperor Wu often saw a comet', '朔折指星木以授帝' means 'Shuo pointed to a star and gave it to the emperor', '帝指彗星应时星没' means 'the emperor pointed to the comet, and it disappeared at the right time', and '时人莫之测也' means 'no one could understand it'.
  • 朔又善啸每曼声长啸辄尘落漫飞朔未死时谓同舍郎曰天下人无能知朔知朔者太王公耳朔卒后武帝得此语即召太王公问之曰尔知东方朔乎公对曰不知公何所能曰颇善星歴帝问诸星皆具在否曰诸星具独不见嵗星十八年今复见耳帝仰天叹曰东方朔生在朕旁十八年而不知是嵗星哉惨然不乐其余事迹多散在别卷此不备载 - A lengthy passage. '朔又善啸' means 'Shuo was also good at whistling', '每曼声长啸辄尘落漫飞' means 'every time he sang a long whistle, dust would fall and fly around', '朔未死时谓同舍郎曰' means 'before Shuo died, he told his fellow chamberlain', '天下人无能知朔知朔者太王公耳' means 'no one in the world knows Shuo except for Taigong', '朔卒后武帝得此语即召太王公问之' means 'after Shuo died, Emperor Wu heard this and summoned Taigong to ask him', '尔知东方朔乎' means 'do you know about Eastern Shuo?', '公对曰不知' means 'Taigong replied, 'I don't know'', '公何所能' means 'what can you do?', '曰颇善星歴' means 'he said, 'I am good at astronomy'', '帝问诸星皆具在否' means 'the emperor asked if all the stars were present', '曰诸星具独不见嵗星十八年今复见耳' means 'he said, 'all the stars are present except for the Star of the Year, which hasn't been seen for 18 years, but it has reappeared now'', '帝仰天叹曰东方朔生在朕旁十八年而不知是嵗星哉' means 'the emperor looked up to heaven and sighed, 'Eastern Shuo lived beside me for 18 years, and I didn't know he was the Star of the Year'', '惨然不乐' means 'he was sad and unhappy', and '其余事迹多散在别卷此不备载' means 'other stories are scattered in other volumes and are not fully recorded here'.

  • 贞观中召至长安于内殿安置 - '贞观中' refers to the reign of Emperor Taizong of Tang during the Zhen Guan era, and '内殿安置' means 'lodged in the inner palace.'
  • 问修习之道对曰臣所修者匹夫之志功不及物利唯一身 - '修习之道' means 'the way of cultivation,' and '匹夫之志' means 'the aspiration of an ordinary person.'
  • 帝王修道一言之利万国防福 - '一言之利' means 'the benefit of a single word,' and '万国防福' means 'the protection and blessing of the entire empire.'

  • 方术服链之门五金八石所难致者必力而求之 - '方术' refers to esoteric practices, and '五金八石' refers to rare minerals and stones.
  • 人有方术未合链施效者必资其药石给其鑪鼎助使成之 - '链施效' means 'to achieve success through alchemy,' and '给其鑪鼎' means 'provide furnaces and tools.'

  • 忽失其尸衣冠不觧如虵蜕耳 - Describes the sudden disappearance of the body, with clothes and cap still intact, likened to a snake shedding its skin.
  • 逺卒后百余日躭亦卒 - States that after the death of the first subject, the second subject died after about a hundred days.

  • 或谓知躭将终故委之而去也 - Another possible interpretation that the first subject knew the second would die and left him.

  • 汝生命应得度世欲取汝以官僚耳 - A statement about the subject's fate and the intention to take him away from officialdom.
  • 然少不知道今气少肉多不得上去当为尸觧如从狗窦中过耳 - Explains the subject's current state and the process of transformation.
  • 告以要言乃委经而去 - Describes the instruction given and the departure of the first subject.
  • 经后忽身体发如火欲得冷水灌之 - Describes Cai Jing's sudden illness, with his body burning like fire and needing cold water.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 人牵经鞭之谓曰麻姑神人也 - Narrative phrase where a person is addressed by someone holding a whip, and is told that 'Maugu is a divine being.' The structure includes a narrative action (牵经鞭之谓曰) that must be rendered carefully to preserve the dialogue and context.
  • 汝何忽谓其爪可爬背耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why did you suddenly say that her claws can scratch your back?' Colloquial and direct expression in Classical Chinese, indicating surprise or disapproval.
  • 但见鞭着经背亦莫见有人持鞭者 - Compound sentence meaning 'But you only saw the whip striking Jing’s back and did not see anyone holding the whip.' Common Classical Chinese structure where the subject is omitted in the second clause for conciseness.
  • 吾鞭不可妄得也 - Statement of authority: 'My whip cannot be obtained lightly.' Idiomatically strong statement reflecting the supernatural or authoritative status of the speaker.
  • 比舍有姓陈者失其名 - Phrase indicating that there is a man from the same neighborhood whose name is unknown. Common structure in Classical Chinese to indicate someone known by surname only.
  • 尝罢县尉 - Indicates that the person used to be a county magistrate (县尉) but has since left that position. Standard biographical expression in Classical Chinese.
  • 乃诣门叩头求乞拜见 - Sequence of actions: went to the door, knelt, begged, and requested an audience. Common way to describe a humble request in Classical Chinese.
  • 临去以一符并一传着以小箱中 - 'On leaving, he gave one talisman and one scroll, placing them in a small box.' Complex sentence with multiple actions and objects, requiring careful parsing.
  • 自出百嵗向上可以禳灾治病者命未终及无罪者 - 'Those who are over a hundred years old, or those who can ward off disasters and cure diseases, or those whose lives have not ended and are innocent.' Grammatically complex sentence with multiple clauses that must be carefully translated.
  • 君以符到其家便愈矣 - 'When you bring the talisman to their home, they will be cured.' Straightforward conditional statement.
  • 君心中亦当知其轻重临时以意治之 - 'You must also know their weight and deal with them appropriately at the time.' Directive to the user of the talisman to use judgment in dealing with different types of spirits.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 麻姑 - Well-known figure in Chinese mythology, associated with immortality, longevity, and divine beings. Appears in classical texts like the *Shanhaijing* and *Lingbao School* texts.
  • 经 - Likely refers to a person named Jing, possibly a Daoist practitioner or deity, though the exact identity is not specified in the text.
  • 鞭 - In Daoist or magical contexts, a whip can be a symbolic or actual object of power, used to command or control spirits or to perform exorcisms.
  • 符 - Common Daoist object used for protection, healing, and exorcism. Talismans were believed to contain magical power and were often inscribed with specific characters or symbols.
  • 传 - Likely refers to a written text or instruction, possibly a Daoist scripture or a magical formula.
  • 县尉 - Local official in ancient China responsible for law and order. The mention of a former county magistrate indicates a shift from bureaucratic to supernatural or spiritual matters.
  • 血食 - Term used to describe evil spirits or demons that feed on blood, often associated with vengeful or malevolent entities.
  • 作祟祸 - Common phrase referring to supernatural interference that causes misfortune or illness.
  • 度世 - Term associated with Daoist and Buddhist immortality, referring to the ability to escape the cycle of rebirth or the limitations of the physical world.
  • 勑吏 - Refers to the act of giving orders to official or bureaucratic figures, possibly in a spiritual or magical context.

Proper Nouns

  • 麻姑 - Daoist deity associated with longevity and immortality. Should be translated as 'Maugu' or 'Maugu, the Divine Being.'
  • 经 - Personal name. Should be translated as 'Jing' or 'Mr. Jing' depending on context.
  • 陈尉 - Person surnamed Chen who was once a county magistrate (县尉). '尉' should be translated as 'magistrate' or 'official.'
  • 符 - Specific term in Daoist and magical contexts. Should be translated as 'talisman.'
  • 传 - Refers to a written text or record, possibly a Daoist scripture or magical formula. Should be translated as 'scroll' or 'record.'
  • 县尉 - Specific bureaucratic title. Should be translated as 'county magistrate' or 'official.'
  • 血食 - Term used in Daoist and folk traditions. Should be translated as 'blood-eating' or 'blood-feeding.'

  • 若顔色欣然則事皆吉若容貌慘戚則事皆凶 - Explains that if Li A's expression was joyful, all matters would be auspicious, and if sorrowful, they would be ominous.
  • 阿含笑者則有大庆防叹者則有深憂 - Describes that if Li A smiled, there would be great joy, and if he sighed, there would be deep sorrow.
  • 如此候之未曾不審也 - Emphasizes that Li A never failed to be accurate in his predictions.
  • 有古强者疑阿異人常親事之 - Introduces an ancient strongman who suspected Li A was a strange person and treated him with respect.
  • 試隨阿還所宿乃在青城山中 - Describes the strongman's curiosity and the revelation that Li A's residence was in Qingcheng Mountain.
  • 強后復欲隨阿去然身未知道恐有虎狼 - Describes the strongman's continued interest in following Li A, despite his fear of tigers and wolves due to his lack of spiritual advancement.
  • 私持其父大刀 - Describes the strongman's preparedness by secretly holding his father's large knife.
  • 阿見而怒 - Describes Li A's reaction to seeing the strongman holding the knife.
  • 汝隨我行那畏虎也 - Describes Li A's reassurance to the strongman that following him would not be scary.
  • 取强刀以擊石刀折壊 - Describes Li A's demonstration of supernatural strength by breaking the strongman's knife with a stone.
  • 强憂刀敗至旦 - Describes the strongman's concern about the broken knife until dawn.
  • 隨出阿問强曰汝愁刀敗也 - Describes Li A's inquiry to the strongman about his concern over the broken knife.
  • 强言實恐父怪怒 - Describes the strongman's fear that his father would be angry.
  • 阿則取刀左手擊地刀復如故 - Describes Li A's demonstration of supernatural power by restoring the broken knife to its original state.
  • 强隨阿還成都 - Describes the strongman's continued journey with Li A back to Chengdu.
  • 未至道逢人奔車 - Describes an encounter before reaching Chengdu.
  • 阿以腳置其車下轹腳皆折 - Describes Li A's demonstration of supernatural power by breaking the wheels of a carriage with his foot.
  • 阿即死 - Describes the sudden death of Li A.
  • 强怖守視之須臾 - Describes the strongman's terror and vigilance after Li A's death.
  • 阿起以手撫腳而復如常 - Describes Li A's resurrection and return to normal.
  • 强年十八見阿年五十許 - Describes the age difference between the strongman and Li A.
  • 强年八十余而阿猶然不異 - Describes Li A's immortality, as he remained unchanged even as the strongman aged.
  • 后語人被昆仑山召當去 - Describes Li A's announcement that he was summoned by Mount Kunlun and would leave.

  • 白日为暝 - Means 'turn day into night,' indicating the power to alter time.
  • 一人能乗云歩虚 - Means 'ride the clouds and walk in emptiness,' indicating the power of flight and ethereal movement.
  • 越海凌波 - Means 'cross the sea and ride the waves,' indicating the power to traverse water.
  • 出入无间 - Means 'enter and exit without obstruction,' indicating the power of seamless movement.
  • 呼吸千里 - Means 'breathe across a thousand miles,' indicating the power of long-distance communication.
  • 一人能入火不灼 - Means 'one can enter fire without being burned,' indicating the power of fire resistance.
  • 入水不濡 - Means 'enter water without getting wet,' indicating the power of water resistance.
  • 刃射 - Means 'arrow shooting,' indicating the power of archery.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不中冬冻不寒夏曝不汗 - Elliptical phrase meaning 'not freezing in winter, not sweating in summer,' describing a state of comfort or balance.
  • 一人能千变万化 - Means 'one person can undergo countless transformations,' emphasizing versatility or magical ability.
  • 恣意所为 - Means 'to act according to one's will,' indicating freedom or unrestrained action.
  • 禽兽草木万物立成 - Means 'to instantly create all things, from animals and plants to all beings,' suggesting omnipotence or divine power.
  • 移山驻景 - Means 'to move mountains and stop the sun,' a metaphor for performing extraordinary feats.
  • 行宫易室 - Means 'to change palaces or rooms at will,' indicating control over one's environment.
  • 防灾度厄 - Means 'to prevent disasters and overcome difficulties,' suggesting protection or salvation.
  • 辟却众害 - Means 'to ward off all harms,' indicating the ability to protect from danger.
  • 煎泥成金 - Means 'to turn mud into gold,' a metaphor for alchemy or transformation.
  • 凝铅为银 - Means 'to turn lead into silver,' another alchemical transformation.
  • 飞腾流珠 - Means 'to fly and flow like pearls,' suggesting movement and transformation.
  • 浮于太清之上 - Means 'to float above the great purity,' indicating a transcendent state.
  • 安乃日夕朝拜供进酒脯 - Means 'he (the king) daily and nightly worshipped and offered wine and meat,' indicating ritual devotion.
  • 各试其向所言千变万化种种异术 - Means 'each tried the various magical arts he had claimed,' indicating testing of abilities.
  • 无有不效 - Means 'nothing was ineffective,' indicating success in all endeavors.
  • 授玉丹经三十六卷 - Means 'he was given the Jade Elixir Scripture in thirty-six volumes,' indicating a sacred text.
  • 自以人莫及也 - Means 'he believed no one could surpass him,' indicating arrogance or confidence.
  • 于时郎中雷被召与之戏 - Means 'at that time, the court official Lei Bei was summoned to play with him,' indicating a challenge or contest.
  • 而被误中迁迁大怒被怖恐为迁所杀 - Means 'but Lei Bei accidentally hit him, causing Qian to rage and Lei Bei to fear being killed,' indicating conflict and fear.
  • 乃求击匈奴以赎罪安闻不听被大惧乃上书于天子 - Means 'he then sought to attack the Xiongnu to atone for his crime, but King An refused to hear him, causing Lei Bei to greatly fear and submit a petition to the emperor,' indicating a plea for mercy.
  • 汉法诸侯壅阏不与击匈奴其罪入死安合当诛 - Means 'According to Han law, if a vassal lord obstructs the attack on the Xiongnu, his crime merits death; King An should be executed,' indicating legal consequences.
  • 武帝素重王不咎但削安二县耳 - Means 'Emperor Wu, who had always esteemed King An, did not blame him but merely reduced his territory by two counties,' indicating leniency.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 恐為安所诛 - Fear of being killed by An, indicating suspicion or paranoia.
  • 共诬告称安谋反 - To collectively falsely accuse An of plotting rebellion, a common tactic in political intrigue.
  • 天子使宗正持节治之 - The emperor sends the Minister of the Royal Clan with a seal to investigate, a formal procedure for high-profile cases.
  • 八公谓安曰可以去矣 - Ba Gong advises An to leave, using '去' in the sense of departing or escaping.
  • 此乃是天之发遣王 - This is heaven's command for the king to depart, a mystical justification for An's actions.
  • 日复一日未能去世也 - Day by day, unable to depart, indicating a delay in the process of leaving.
  • 大祭埋金地中 - A grand ritual involving burying gold in the ground, likely a magical or sacrificial act.
  • 白日升天 - To ascend to heaven during the day, a mythological event often associated with immortality.
  • 八公告安曰夫有借之人被人诬告者其诬人当即死灭 - Ba Gong tells An that those who borrow others' accusations will die immediately, a warning about the consequences of false accusations.
  • 伍被等今当伏诛矣 - Wu Bei and others will now be executed, indicating the outcome of the accusations.
  • 宗正以失安所在推问云王仙去矣 - The Minister of the Royal Clan reports that An has disappeared, suggesting he has become a 'flying immortal.'
  • 天子怅然 - The emperor is disappointed or sorrowful, showing his emotional response.
  • 讽使廷尉张汤奏伍被云为画计乃诛二被九族一如八公之言也 - The emperor indirectly orders the Chief Justice Zhang Tang to report that Wu Bei and his family were executed as per Ba Gong's advice.
  • 汉史袐之不言安得神仙之道 - The Han history keeps this secret and does not mention An's attainment of the way of immortality, indicating a deliberate omission.
  • 恐后世人主当废万几而竞求于安道 - Fearing that future emperors might neglect state affairs in pursuit of An's methods, showing a concern about the influence of An's teachings.
  • 乃言安得罪后自杀非得仙也 - Therefore, it is said that An committed suicide after being punished, rather than achieving immortality, a different narrative from the one involving Ba Gong.
  • 按左吴记云安临去欲诛二被八公諌曰不可仙去不欲害行虫况于人乎安乃止 - According to the *Zuo Wu Ji*, An wanted to kill Wu Bei and others before leaving, but Ba Gong advised against it, saying that ascending to heaven does not involve harming living beings, so An stopped.
  • 又问八公曰可得将素所交亲俱至 - An asks Ba Gong if he can take his close friends and family with him, showing a desire to include his loved ones in his ascension.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 安 - Refers to Liu An, a prince of the Han dynasty known for his interest in alchemy and immortality.

  • 廷尉 - The Chief Justice, the highest judicial official in the Han dynasty.
  • 张汤 - Zhang Tang, a famous judge and politician of the Han dynasty.
  • 伍被 - Wu Bei, a scholar and advisor to Liu An, who was involved in the plot against him.
  • 汉史 - The official history of the Han dynasty, which may have been edited to omit certain details.
  • 左吴记 - The *Zuo Wu Ji* (Left Wu's Record), a historical text that provides additional details about Liu An's life and death.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 恐为安所诛乃共诬告称安谋反 - Fearing they would be killed by An, they collectively falsely accused An of plotting rebellion.
  • 天子使宗正持节治之 - The emperor sent the Minister of the Royal Clan with a seal to investigate the matter.
  • 八公谓安曰可以去矣此乃是天之发遣王 - Ba Gong told An, 'You can leave now; this is heaven's command for the king.'
  • 日复一日未能去世也 - Day by day, unable to depart, indicating a delay in the process of leaving.
  • 大祭埋金地中即白日升天 - A grand ritual involving burying gold in the ground, followed by ascending to heaven during the day.
  • 八公告安曰夫有借之人被人诬告者其诬人当即死灭伍被等今当伏诛矣 - Ba Gong told An that those who borrow others' accusations will die immediately, and Wu Bei and others will now be executed.
  • 宗正以失安所在推问云王仙去矣 - The Minister of the Royal Clan reported that An had disappeared, suggesting he had become a 'flying immortal.'
  • 天子怅然乃讽使廷尉张汤奏伍被云为画计乃诛二被九族一如八公之言也 - The emperor was disappointed and indirectly ordered the Chief Justice Zhang Tang to report that Wu Bei and his family were executed as per Ba Gong's advice.
  • 汉史袐之不言安得神仙之道 - The Han history kept this secret and did not mention An's attainment of the way of immortality.
  • 恐后世人主当废万几而竞求于安道 - Fearing that future emperors might neglect state affairs in pursuit of An's methods.
  • 乃言安得罪后自杀非得仙也 - Therefore, it is said that An committed suicide after being punished, rather than achieving immortality.
  • 按左吴记云安临去欲诛二被八公諌曰不可仙去不欲害行虫况于人乎安乃止 - According to the *Zuo Wu Ji*, An wanted to kill Wu Bei and others before leaving, but Ba Gong advised against it, saying that ascending to heaven does not involve harming living beings, so An stopped.
  • 又问八公曰可得将素所交亲俱至 - An asked Ba Gong if he could take his close friends and family with him.

  • 亦冀遇八公 - A phrase meaning 'hoping to meet the Eight Immortals.'

  • 少生富贵之门而不好荣贵 - Classical expression meaning 'born into a wealthy family but not desiring honor and wealth,' highlighting the protagonist's unique character.
  • 唯専务道术 - Means 'only devoted to Daoist practices,' emphasizing the character's dedication to spiritual pursuits.
  • 闻马鸣生得度世之道乃寻求之 - Means 'heard that Master Ma Ming had the way to transcend the world and sought him out,' indicating the protagonist's quest for spiritual enlightenment.
  • 遂得相见便执奴仆之役亲运履之劳 - Means 'and upon meeting, he willingly took on the role of a servant, performing menial tasks,' showing the character's humility and dedication.
  • 鸣生不教以度世之法但日夕别与之髙谈论当世之事治农田之业 - Means 'Master Ma Ming did not teach him the way to transcend the world but instead talked with him about current affairs and farming,' indicating the unconventional nature of their relationship.
  • 如此十余年长生不懈 - Means 'for over ten years, Longsheng remained steadfast,' emphasizing the character's persistence.
  • 同时共事鸣生者十二人皆悉归去唯长生执礼弥肃 - Means 'twelve others who served Master Ma Ming all left, but Longsheng remained even more respectful,' highlighting the character's exceptional dedication.
  • 乃将入青城山中煮黄土为金以示之 - Means 'he then took him into Qingcheng Mountain, where he turned clay into gold to show him,' indicating a miraculous demonstration.
  • 立坛西面乃以太清神丹经授之 - Means 'he set up an altar facing west and gave him the *Taiqing Shen Dan Jing* (Great Clarity Divine Elixir Scripture),' showing the transmission of sacred knowledge.
  • 鸣生别去长生乃归合之丹成服半剂不尽即升天 - Means 'Master Ma Ming left, and Longsheng returned home, completed the elixir, and after consuming half of it, ascended to heaven,' indicating the culmination of his spiritual journey.

  • 唯升与王长嘿然 - 'Only Seng and Wang Chang remained silent,' where '嘿然' indicates silence or contemplation.
  • 陵遂投空不落 - 'Ling then jumped into the void and did not fall,' where '遂' indicates a sequence of actions.
  • 桃上失陵所在 - 'The peach tree lost track of Ling's location,' where '失' means to lose or forget.
  • 四方皆仰上则连天下则无底往无道路莫不惊叹悲涕 - Complex phrase meaning 'all directions looked up, above it was connected to heaven, below it was bottomless, there were no paths, everyone was amazed and cried.'
  • 唯升长二人良乆乃相谓曰 - 'Only Seng and Chang waited for a long time before saying to each other,' where '良乆' means a long time.
  • 师则父也自投于不测之崖吾何以自安 - Rhetorical question: 'A teacher is like a father, we have thrown ourselves into an unpredictable cliff, how can we feel at ease?' where '不测之崖' means an unpredictable or dangerous cliff.
  • 乃俱投身而下 - 'Then they both jumped down,' where '俱' means together.
  • 正堕陵前见陵坐局脚牀斗帐中 - 'They landed right in front of Ling, who was sitting on a small bed with a canopy,' where '局脚牀' is a small bed and '斗帐' is a canopy.
  • 见升长二人笑曰 - 'They saw Seng and Chang laughing and said,' where '笑曰' introduces direct speech.
  • 吾知汝来乃授二人道毕三日乃还归治旧舎 - 'I knew you would come and taught you the way, after three days, you returned to manage your old residence,' where '道' means the way or method.
  • 诸弟子惊悲不息 - 'The other disciples were shocked and grieving without ceasing,' where '不息' means without ceasing.
  • 后陵与升长三人皆白日升天而去 - 'Later, Ling and Seng and Chang all ascended to heaven during the day and left,' where '白日升天' means ascending to heaven during the day.
  • 初陵入蜀山合丹半剂虽未冲举已成地仙故欲化作七试以度赵升乃如其志也 - Complex phrase explaining Ling's motivation: 'Initially, Ling entered the Shu mountains, mixed half a dose of elixir, although he had not yet ascended, he had already become a terrestrial immortal, so he wanted to transform into seven trials to guide Zhao Sheng, thus fulfilling his wish,' where '冲举' means ascending and '地仙' means terrestrial immortal.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 赵升 - Zhao Sheng, a disciple of the immortal Ling Bao.
  • 王长 - Wang Chang, another disciple of the immortal Ling Bao.
  • 陵 - Ling Bao, a famous immortal in Chinese mythology.
  • 蜀山 - Shu mountains, a mythical mountain range in Chinese folklore.
  • 地仙 - Terrestrial immortal, a type of immortal who has achieved immortality but remains on Earth.
  • 七试 - Seven trials, a series of tests or challenges.
  • 神仙传 - *Shenxian Zhuan* (Records of the Immortals), a collection of stories about immortals in Chinese mythology.
