Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • The *Taiping Guangji* is a vast collection of stories from various sources, including historical records, legends, and folk tales. It is a key source for understanding Chinese mythology and folklore.
  • The *Siku Quanshu* is one of the most important collections of Chinese literature, commissioned by Emperor Qianlong. It includes a wide range of texts from different periods and genres.
  • Immortals in Daoist tradition are often depicted as beings who have transcended the limitations of the physical world through spiritual cultivation and alchemy.
  • Li Shaojun is a legendary figure in Daoist lore, often associated with alchemy and the pursuit of immortality. His story is often used to illustrate the Daoist ideal of transcending the limitations of the physical world.
  • Emperor Wu of Han was a powerful and influential ruler who sought immortality through the use of alchemists and magicians. His reign is often associated with the height of the Han Dynasty's power and cultural achievements.
  • These were practitioners of Daoist alchemy and magic, often sought by emperors for their knowledge of immortality and other supernatural abilities.
  • A legendary Daoist figure known for his longevity and alchemical knowledge. He is often associated with the pursuit of immortality and the use of alchemical techniques.
  • This refers to the method of creating elixirs of immortality using an alchemical furnace. The furnace is a key tool in Daoist alchemy, used to transform base materials into elixirs of immortality.
  • In this context, this phrase refers to the end of life or the approach of death, but it is also used to describe the transition from the physical world to the spiritual realm in Daoist thought.
  • This phrase highlights the challenges faced by those who seek immortality, as the process often requires significant resources and wealth.
  • This refers to manual labor, often associated with the Daoist ideal of living a simple and humble life in harmony with nature.
  • The emperor was considered the supreme ruler of China, and his power was often associated with the divine right to rule. Emperor Wu's pursuit of immortality reflects his desire to extend his reign and achieve eternal life.
  • This refers to the preparation of medicine or elixirs, often associated with the pursuit of immortality in Daoist alchemy.
  • This phrase means 'to act freely' or 'to do as one pleases,' reflecting the Daoist ideal of living in harmony with nature and following one's own path.
  • Cinnabar is a mineral used in alchemy to create elixirs of immortality. It is often associated with the transformation of base materials into something more refined and valuable.
  • Gold is often associated with immortality in alchemical texts, as it is believed to have the power to transform and purify.
  • This refers to the process of ascending to immortality, often associated with the transformation of the physical body into a spiritual form.
  • This refers to wandering by the sea, often associated with Daoist immortals who live in remote, mystical places. The sea is often associated with the Daoist ideal of transcendence and the pursuit of immortality.
  • This is a title or rank, likely referring to a noble or official in the court of Emperor Wu. The presence of a high-ranking official in the story highlights the importance of immortality in the imperial court.
  • This refers to an ancient bronze vessel, often used in rituals or as a symbol of antiquity. The discovery of such an artifact is often associated with the pursuit of immortality, as it is believed to hold the secrets of the past.
  • Duke Huan of Qi was a famous ruler of the state of Qi during the Spring and Autumn period. His reign is often associated with the height of the state's power and cultural achievements.
  • This refers to a place where the Duke slept or rested, often associated with his personal quarters. The discovery of such an artifact is often associated with the pursuit of immortality, as it is believed to hold the secrets of the past.
  • This refers to inscriptions or carvings on the bronze vessel, often associated with the pursuit of immortality, as they are believed to hold the secrets of the past.
  • This refers to someone who has lived for hundreds of years, often associated with immortals or long-lived Daoists. The presence of such a person in the story highlights the importance of immortality in Daoist thought.
  • This refers to the appearance or countenance of the person, often associated with the Daoist ideal of living a simple and humble life in harmony with nature.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 色肌肤甚有光泽口齿如童子 - Describes someone with a radiant complexion and teeth as fresh as a child's, emphasizing youthfulness and vitality.
  • 王公贵人闻其能令人不死莫不仰慕 - Refers to nobles and high-ranking officials who, upon hearing that someone can grant immortality, all admire and respect them.
  • 所遗金钱山积 - Describes the accumulation of wealth left behind, suggesting a large amount of money.
  • 丹成谓帝曰 - Means 'After the elixir was prepared, Shaojun told the emperor.'
  • 陛下不能绝骄奢遣声色杀伐不止喜怒不胜 - Criticizes the emperor for not being able to curb his indulgence in luxury, entertainment, and warfare, and for being unable to control his emotions.
  • 神丹大道未可得成 - Indicates that the divine elixir and the path to immortality are not yet attainable.
  • 乃以少药方与帝 - Means 'He then gave the emperor a small formula for a medicine.'
  • 少君便称疾 - Indicates that Shaojun then claimed to be ill.
  • 少君乃病困帝往视之并使人受其方事未竟而卒 - Describes Shaojun becoming seriously ill, the emperor visiting him, and attempting to receive the formula before Shaojun's death.
  • 帝曰少君不死故化去耳 - Indicates that the emperor believed Shaojun had not died but had transformed into something else.
  • 帝犹憎叹恨求少君不勤也 - Expresses the emperor's continued regret and desire to find Shaojun.
  • 初少君与朝议郎董仲躬相亲爱 - Indicates that initially, Shaojun was close friends with an official named Dong Zhonggong.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 少君 - Refers to a legendary alchemist or sorcerer who claimed to have the knowledge of immortality.
  • 王公贵人 - Refers to nobles and high-ranking officials in ancient China.
  • 太乙 - Refers to the Heavenly Lord, a deity in Chinese mythology.
  • 朝议郎 - Refers to a low-ranking official in the imperial court.
  • 董仲躬 - Refers to an official named Dong Zhonggong, who was a friend of Shaojun.

Proper Nouns

  • 少君 - The legendary alchemist or sorcerer.
  • 太乙 - The Heavenly Lord.
  • 朝议郎 - A low-ranking official in the imperial court.
  • 董仲躬 - An official named Dong Zhonggong.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 躬宿有疾 - Classical expression meaning 'the person (躬宿) has an illness.'
  • 体枯气少 - Describes a physical state of being emaciated and having weak vitality.
  • 少君乃与其成药二剂 - Formal expression indicating that 'the young master (少君) then prepared two doses of medicine.'
  • 戊己之草后土脂黄精根兽沈昉先莠之根百卉花酿亥月上旬合煎铜器中 - Highly technical and archaic description of ingredients and preparation method, including '戊己之草' (a type of grass), '后土脂' (fatty substance from the earth), '黄精根' (root of Polygonatum), '兽沈昉先莠之根' (roots of a wild animal), '百卉花酿' (fermented flowers), and '亥月上旬' (the first ten days of the Pig month).
  • 使童子沐浴洁净调其汤火使合成鸡子三枚为程 - Detailed instruction on preparing the medicine, involving a child (童子) performing a ritual cleansing and adjusting the heat to form three egg-sized pills.
  • 身体便轻 - Means 'the body becomes lighter.'
  • 齿落更生 - Means 'teeth fall out and grow back.'
  • 年夀长而不复倾 - Means 'lifespan extends without further decline.'
  • 刚直博学五经 - Describes a person who is upright, knowledgeable, and well-versed in the Five Classics.
  • 不逹道术 - Means 'does not understand the way of Daoist practices.'
  • 笑世人服药学道频上书谏武帝 - Means 'mocks the world for taking medicine and practicing Daoism, frequently submitting memorials to Emperor Wu.'
  • 以为人生则命衰老有常非道术所能延 - Means 'believes that aging and death are natural and cannot be extended by Daoist practices.'
  • 意虽见其有异将为天性非术所致 - Means 'thinks that any differences are due to natural disposition rather than technique.'
  • 得药竟不服 - Means 'received the medicine but did not take it.'
  • 又不问其方 - Means 'also did not inquire about the formula.'
  • 少君去后数月仲躬病甚 - Means 'after the young master left, several months later, the person became seriously ill.'
  • 常闻武帝説前夣恨惜少君 - Means 'often heard Emperor Wu speak of his regret for the young master.'
  • 仲躬忆少君所留药试服之 - Means 'the person remembered the medicine left by the young master and tried it.'
  • 未半乃身体轻壮其病顿愈 - Means 'after taking half, the body became lighter and stronger, and the illness was suddenly cured.'
  • 服尽气力如年少时 - Means 'after finishing the medicine, the strength returned as if in youth.'
  • 乃信有长生不死之道 - Means 'then believed in the way of immortality.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 戊己之草 - A type of grass used in traditional Chinese medicine, often associated with alchemical practices.
  • 后土脂 - A fatty substance from the earth, possibly referring to a mineral or plant extract.
  • 黄精根 - The root of Polygonatum, a common herb in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 兽沈昉先莠之根 - Roots of a wild animal, possibly referring to a specific herb or plant.
  • 百卉花酿 - Fermented flowers, likely a mixture of various herbs and flowers.
  • 亥月上旬 - The first ten days of the Pig month (the tenth month in the lunar calendar).

  • 无所见唯有竹筒一枚 - Nothing was seen, only one bamboo tube.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 孔元方許昌人也 - Standard biographical opening, meaning 'Kong Yuanfang is from Xuchang.'
  • 常服松脂伏苓松實等藥 - Means 'often takes medicines such as pine resin, *fu ling* (poria), and pine nuts.'
  • 老而益少 - Common phrase meaning 'becomes younger with age.'
  • 容如四十許人 - Means 'looks like someone in their forties.'
  • 郄元节左元放皆為親友 - Means 'Qie Yuanjie and Zuo Yuanfang are all his close friends.'
  • 俱棄五經當世之人事専修道术 - Means 'they all abandon the Five Classics and worldly affairs, devoting themselves exclusively to Daoist practices.'
  • 元方仁慈惡衣蔬食 - Means 'Kong Yuanfang is kind-hearted, wearing shabby clothes and eating simple food.'
  • 飲酒不過一升 - Means 'drinks no more than one sheng (unit of measurement) of wine.'
  • 年有七十余嵗 - Means 'he is over seventy years old.'
  • 道家或請元方防同飲酒 - Means 'Daoists sometimes invite Kong Yuanfang to join them in drinking.'
  • 次至元方 - Means 'when it comes to Kong Yuanfang.'
  • 作一令以杖拄地 - Means 'he makes a command and places his staff on the ground.'
  • 乃手把杖倒竖头在下足向上 - Means 'then he takes the staff and holds it upside down, with the head pointing downward and the foot pointing upward.'
  • 以一手持杯倒饮 - Means 'with one hand holding a cup, he drinks upside down.'
  • 人莫能为也 - Means 'no one else can do this.'
  • 元方有妻子不畜余财 - Means 'Kong Yuanfang has a wife and children but does not accumulate extra wealth.'
  • 颇种五谷 - Means 'he grows some grains.'
  • 时失火 - Means 'once there was a fire.'
  • 诸人并来救之 - Means 'everyone came to help.'
  • 出屋下衣粮牀几 - Means 'they took out the clothes, food, bed, and tables from under the house.'
  • 元方都不救唯箕踞篱下视火 - Means 'Kong Yuanfang did not help at all, sitting cross-legged under the fence watching the fire.'
  • 其妻促使元方助収物 - Means 'his wife urged him to help gather the things.'
  • 何用惜此 - Means 'why should we be so attached to these things?'
  • 凿水邉岸作一窟室方广丈余 - Means 'he dug into the bank of the water to make a cave room that is one zhang (unit of measurement) wide and long.'
  • 元方入其中断谷 - Means 'Kong Yuanfang entered it and stopped eating.'
  • 或一月 - Means 'sometimes for a month.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 两月乃复还 - Literally means 'after two months returned,' indicating a specific period of time.
  • 家人亦不得往来窟前 - Means 'family members could not visit the cave entrance,' emphasizing the isolation.
  • 有一栢生道后草间委曲蔽 - Describes a pine tree growing behind the path, hidden by grass and winding around, providing cover.
  • 弟子有急欲诣元方窟室者皆莫能知 - Means 'disciples who urgently wanted to reach Yuanfang's cave room could not find it,' highlighting the difficulty in locating the cave.
  • 后东方有一少年姓冯名遇好道伺元方便寻窟室得见 - Describes a young man from the east named Feng Yu, who was devoted to the Dao, waited for Yuanfang, and found the cave room.
  • 人皆来不能见我汝得见似可教也 - Means 'everyone else can't see me, but you can, so you seem to be teachable,' indicating a special recognition.
  • 乃以素书二卷授之曰此道之要言也 - Means 'he then gave him two scrolls of white writing, saying, 'These are the essential words of the Dao,' emphasizing the importance of the teachings.
  • 四十年得传一人世无其人不得以年限足故妄授 - Means 'every forty years, one person can be taught; if there is no one worthy, do not teach just because the time is up,' stressing the importance of finding the right student.
  • 若四十年无所授者即八十年而有二人可授者即顿接二人可授不授为闭天道不可授而授为泄天道皆殃及子孙 - Means 'if after forty years no one is taught, then after eighty years, if two people are worthy, teach both; not teaching when you should is closing the way of heaven, and teaching when you shouldn't is leaking the way of heaven, both of which will bring disaster to your descendants,' emphasizing the consequences of improper teaching.
  • 乃委妻子入西岳后五十余年暂还乡里时人尚有识之者 - Means 'he then left his wife and children to enter the Western Mountain, and after more than fifty years, he briefly returned to his hometown, where some people still recognized him,' indicating a long absence and eventual return.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 元方 - Likely refers to a historical figure or a legendary figure known for his Daoist practices and teachings.
  • 西岳 - Refers to the Western Mountain, a significant location in Chinese mythology and Daoism, often associated with immortality and spiritual practice.
  • 神仙传 - Refers to the 'Xiansheng Zhuan,' a collection of biographies of immortals and legendary figures in Chinese folklore and Daoist literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 元方 - A name or title of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 西岳 - The Western Mountain.
  • 神仙传 - The 'Xiansheng Zhuan' (Biographies of Immortals).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 王烈者字長休邯鄲人也 - Standard biographical formula in Classical Chinese, meaning 'Wang Lie, whose courtesy name was Changxiu, was from Handan.'
  • 猶有少容 - Idiom meaning 'still had a youthful appearance' despite extreme age.
  • 登山歴險行歩如飛 - Poetic way to describe physical agility.
  • 學无不览 - Hyperbolic expression emphasizing erudition.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 焦先者字孝然河东人也 - Standard biographical introduction in Classical Chinese, meaning 'Jiao Xian, courtesy name Xiao Ran, was from Hedong.'
  • 常食白石以分与人 - Literal meaning 'often ate white stones and distributed them to others,' indicating Jiao Xian shared his unusual diet with others.
  • 熟煮如芋食之 - Meaning 'cooked them until they were as soft as yams and ate them,' describing the preparation of the white stones.
  • 日日入山伐薪以施人 - Meaning 'every day he went into the mountains to cut wood and gave it to others,' indicating his daily routine of helping others.
  • 先自村头一家起周而复始 - Meaning 'Jiao started from the first house at the head of the village and went around in a circle,' describing his methodical approach to distributing his resources.
  • 负薪来如不见人 - Meaning 'when carrying firewood, he acted as if he did not see people,' indicating his reclusive and detached behavior.
  • 私置于门间便去 - Meaning 'he secretly placed the firewood at the door and then left,' showing his unobtrusive and selfless nature.
  • 连年如此 - Meaning 'he did this year after year,' emphasizing the consistency of his actions.
  • 及魏受禅居河之湄结草为庵独止其中 - Meaning 'when Cao Wei received the Mandate of Heaven (i.e., when the Han dynasty was replaced by the Wei dynasty), Jiao lived by the river, built a thatched hut, and lived alone in it,' describing his lifestyle during a significant historical event.
  • 不设床席以草褥衬坐 - Meaning 'he did not set up beds or mats but used a straw mat to sit on,' indicating his simple and austere living conditions.
  • 其身垢汚浊如泥潦 - Meaning 'his body was dirty and muddy like rainwater,' describing his unkempt appearance.
  • 或数日一食 - Meaning 'sometimes he ate only once every few days,' indicating his sparse diet.
  • 行不由径不与女人交游 - Meaning 'he did not walk on paths and did not associate with women,' suggesting his avoidance of conventional social norms.
  • 衣则卖薪以买故衣着之 - Meaning 'when his clothes were tattered, he sold firewood to buy old clothes and wore them,' showing his resourcefulness and frugality.
  • 冬夏 - Meaning 'winter and summer,' indicating the passage of time and the consistency of his lifestyle throughout the year.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 焦先 - A historical figure known for his reclusive and ascetic lifestyle.

  • 河之湄 - Refers to the banks of the Yellow River, a major geographical feature in Chinese history.
  • 结草为庵 - Describes a simple, thatched hut, a common dwelling for recluses and ascetics.
  • 不设床席以草褥衬坐 - Reflects the austere and minimalist lifestyle of recluses and ascetics in Chinese culture.
  • 其身垢汚浊如泥潦 - Describes the unkempt and dirty appearance often associated with recluses and ascetics.
  • 行不由径不与女人交游 - Reflects the ascetic and reclusive lifestyle, avoiding conventional social interactions.

  • 发狂 - Going mad, indicating a severe emotional or mental breakdown.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 妻登時死良久乃蘇 - Compound expression meaning 'the wife died immediately, but after a long time she revived.' Reflects a common narrative structure in classical Chinese where a character appears to die but later recovers, often as a result of supernatural intervention.
  • 云见府君家先捉者大怒 - '云' is likely a narrative marker meaning 'said' or 'reported,' and '见府君家先捉者大怒' translates to 'saw the official's family who had previously captured [her] and became greatly angry.' This phrase shows a narrative shift from the wife's recovery to her recounting of an encounter.
  • 汝何故犯神仙尊官使我见收 - Direct question: 'Why did you commit a crime against a divine official, causing me to be captured?' Reflects a supernatural authority and a moral or legal accusation.
  • 今当来杀汝 - Threat: 'Now I shall come to kill you.' This is a clear expression of impending doom or retribution.
  • 其后一月府君夫妇男皆卒 - 'After a month, the official's wife, husband, and son all died.' This is a concise but dramatic statement of a tragic event.
  • 府掾王珍数得见数承顔色懽然 - 'The official's subordinate Wang Zhen was often allowed to see [the spirit], and he was always happy in its presence.' The repetition of '数' (often) emphasizes the frequency of the interaction and the positive emotional response.
  • 时伏地叩头请问根学仙时本末 - 'At that time, he fell to the ground and knelt, asking Root about the beginning and end of his study of immortality.' This is a formal and respectful gesture of inquiry, common in classical texts involving spiritual or supernatural beings.
  • 吾昔入山精思无所不到 - 'I once entered the mountains and meditated with great intensity, reaching everywhere.' This is a poetic and metaphorical way of expressing deep spiritual practice.
  • 后如华隂山见一人乗白鹿车从者十余人左右玉女四人执采旄之节 - Complex description of a vision or encounter: 'Later, I went to Huayin Mountain and saw a man riding a white deer carriage, with over ten followers, and four jade women on either side holding colorful banners.' This is a typical classical Chinese description of a divine or supernatural figure.
  • 皆年十五六余载拜稽首求乞一言 - 'All were about fifteen or sixteen years old, and after many years, I knelt and begged for a single word.' This is a formal and respectful request, common in spiritual or religious contexts.
  • 尔闻有韩众否 - 'Have you heard of Han Zhong?' This is a direct question, introducing a historical or mythological figure.
  • 我是也 - 'I am [Han Zhong].' A direct and dramatic affirmation, indicating the identity of the supernatural figure.
  • 愿见哀怜赐其要诀 - 'I wish you would show mercy and grant me the essential teachings.' This is a formal and respectful request for spiritual instruction.
  • 神未肯告余 - 'The deity did not wish to tell me.' This indicates a refusal or reluctance on the part of the supernatural being.
  • 余乃流 - 'I then became lost' or 'I then wandered.' This is an incomplete or abrupt ending, possibly due to the original text being cut off or incomplete.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 府君 - A title used for a local official or magistrate, often associated with the administration of justice and local governance in ancient China.
  • 神仙尊官 - 'Divine official' or 'official of the immortals,' referring to a supernatural authority or deity in the context of Daoist or spiritual traditions.
  • 华隂山 - Huayin Mountain, a real mountain in Shaanxi Province, often associated with Daoist and mythological traditions in Chinese literature.
  • 白鹿车 - A white deer carriage, a common motif in Chinese mythology and Daoist texts, symbolizing a divine or immortal being.
  • 玉女 - 'Jade women,' a term for beautiful female attendants or spirits, often associated with deities or immortals in Daoist and mythological contexts.
  • 采旄之节 - 'Colorful banners with旄 (feathered staffs),' likely symbols of authority or divine office.
  • 韩众 - A historical or mythological figure, possibly a Daoist immortal or sage. In Daoist tradition, Han Zhong (韩众) is sometimes associated with the pursuit of immortality and is mentioned in classical texts.
  • 学仙 - 'Learning immortality' or 'studying the Dao,' a central theme in Daoist and alchemical texts.
  • 要诀 - 'Essential teachings' or 'crucial secrets,' often referring to the key knowledge or practices required for achieving immortality or spiritual transcendence.

Proper Nouns

  • 府君 - A title for a local official or magistrate; should be translated consistently as 'the official' or 'the magistrate.'
  • 王珍 - A proper name, likely a subordinate or official; should be rendered as 'Wang Zhen.'
  • 根 - A personal name or title; should be translated consistently as 'Root' or 'Gen.'
  • 韩众 - A historical or mythological figure; should be translated as 'Han Zhong' or 'Han Zhong the Immortal.'
  • 华隂山 - A proper place name; should be translated as 'Huayin Mountain.'
  • 玉女 - A term for a divine female attendant or spirit; should be translated consistently as 'jade women' or 'beauties of jade.'
  • 采旄之节 - A term for a ceremonial banner or staff; should be translated as 'colorful banners with feathered staffs' or 'banners with旄 (feathered staffs).'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 涕自搏重請神人曰 - Narrative phrase meaning 'the person wept and repeatedly implored the divine being, saying...'
  • 坐吾將告汝 - Command or invitation meaning 'sit, I will tell you...'
  • 汝有仙骨故得見吾耳 - Causal statement meaning 'you have the bone of a sage/immortal, hence you are able to see me.'
  • 服藥行氣不得其力 - Phrase meaning 'taking medicine and practicing qi cultivation do not yield their full power.'
  • 必欲長生且先治病十二年乃可服仙藥耳 - Conditional statement: 'if you desire immortality, you must first treat your illness for twelve years before you can take the elixir of immortality.'
  • 夫仙道有升天蹑云者 - Classification of types of immortality: 'the path of immortality includes those who ascend to heaven and tread clouds...'
  • 有服食不死者 - Another type of immortal: 'those who consume elixirs and do not die.'
  • 有尸解而仙者 - Another method of becoming immortal: 'those who become immortal through the method of *shijie* (corpse transformation).'
  • 凡修仙道要在服藥 - General principle: 'in all cultivation of immortality, the key lies in taking medicine.'
  • 藥有上下仙有數品 - Classification: 'medicines have different levels, and immortality has multiple ranks.'
  • 不知房中之事及行氣導引並神藥者亦不能仙也 - Negation: 'those who do not know the art of inner alchemy (*fangzhong*), breathing techniques, and guiding practices, along with the divine medicines, cannot become immortal.'
  • 藥之上者有九轉還丹太乙金液 - Description of the highest medicines: 'the highest medicines include the *jiuzhuan huan dan* (nine-refined elixir) and *Taiyi jin ye* (Great One Golden Elixir).'
  • 服之皆立登天不積日月矣 - Result of taking the highest elixirs: 'those who take them ascend to heaven immediately, without the accumulation of days and months.'
  • 其次有云母雄黃之屬 - Classification: 'the next level includes substances like cloud mother (cloudstone) and realgar (雄黄).'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 仙骨 - Term from Daoist tradition referring to the physical or spiritual constitution of someone destined for immortality.
  • 仙道 - Daoist path to immortality or transcendence.
  • 服藥行氣 - Practices of Daoist alchemy and breathing exercises to cultivate life force (*qi*).
  • 九轉還丹 - Legendary Daoist elixir said to be refined through nine transformations, capable of granting immortality.
  • 太乙金液 - Another name for the 'Great One Golden Elixir' (*Taiyi jin ye*), a powerful Daoist elixir.
  • 云母 - Mineral used in Daoist alchemy, often associated with longevity.
  • 雄黄 - Realgar, a red mineral used in Daoist medicine and alchemy.
  • 房中之事 - Refers to sexual practices in Daoist inner alchemy, also known as *fangzhong*.
  • 行氣導引 - Techniques of breath control and physical exercises to regulate *qi* and prolong life.
  • 尸解 - Daoist method of achieving immortality where the body is left behind, often metaphorically described as 'dying in the flesh but living in the spirit.'
  • 草木諸藥 - Herbal medicines derived from plants, used in Daoist and traditional Chinese medicine for health and longevity, but not for full immortality.

Proper Nouns

  • 九轉還丹 - *Jiu Zhuan Huan Dan* (Nine-Refined Elixir), a legendary Daoist alchemical elixir.
  • 太乙金液 - *Taiyi Jin Ye* (Great One Golden Elixir), another name for the *Jiu Zhuan Huan Dan* or a related elixir.
  • 云母 - *Yun Mu* (Cloud Mother), a mineral used in Daoist alchemy.
  • 雄黄 - *Xionghuang* (Realgar), a red mineral used in alchemy and medicine.
  • 房中之事 - *Fangzhong* (Inner Alchemy), a term referring to sexual practices in Daoist cultivation.
  • 行氣導引 - *Xingqi Daoyin* (Breathing and Guiding), Daoist practices for cultivating *qi*.
  • 尸解 - *Shijie* (Corpse Transformation), a Daoist method of achieving immortality.
  • 草木諸藥 - *Caomu Zhuyao* (Herbal Medicines), a category of traditional Chinese medicines.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 久賴也余頓首曰 - Formal expression of respect, meaning 'I, Jiu Lai, bow deeply and say.'
  • 今日敎乃天也 - Means 'Today’s instruction is from heaven,' emphasizing the divine or supreme nature of the teaching.
  • 神人曰 - Means 'the divine person said,' referring to a being with supernatural qualities.
  • 必欲长生先去三尸 - Means 'If you wish to live long, first remove the Three Corpses,' referring to a Daoist practice.
  • 三尸去即志意定嗜欲除也 - Means 'When the Three Corpses are removed, the will is stabilized and desires are eliminated.'
  • 乃以神方五篇见授云 - Means 'He then gave me five divine formulas,' referring to a set of instructions or recipes.
  • 伏尸常以月朢晦朔上天白人罪过 - Means 'The Three Corpses report human sins to heaven on the full moon, new moon, and first day of the month.'
  • 司命夺人算使人不寿 - Means 'The Keeper of Fate takes away one’s years, making them not live long.'
  • 人身中神欲得人生而尸欲得人死 - Means 'The spirit in the body wants life, while the Three Corpses want death.'
  • 人死则神散无形之中而成 - Means 'When a person dies, the spirit disperses and becomes a ghost.'
  • 祭祀之则得歆飨 - Means 'If they are worshipped, they receive offerings.'
  • 梦与恶人鬭争 - Means 'Dreaming of fighting with bad people,' which is a metaphor for the struggle between the spirit and the Three Corpses.
  • 此乃尸与神相战也 - Means 'This is the Three Corpses fighting against the spirit.'
  • 余乃从其言合服之遂以得仙珍 - Means 'I followed his words, took the formulas, and thus became a divine treasure.'
  • 又每见根书符了有所呼召 - Means 'Also, I often see Gen write talismans and call upon something.'

  • 莫测其端也 - Means 'It is impossible to understand the beginning.'
  • 根乃敎珍守一行气存神坐三纲六纪谢过上名之法 - Means 'Gen then taught Zhen to practice one breath, conserve the spirit, sit in meditation, and follow the Three Bonds and Six Ordinances to repent and ascend.'
  • 根后入鸡头山仙去 - Means 'Gen later entered Chicken Head Mountain and ascended to immortality.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汝辈愚人 - Refers to 'you foolish people' or 'you ignorant ones,' a direct address to a group of people considered unintelligent.
  • 道学未得而欲杀之 - Means 'not having attained the Way but wanting to kill him,' indicating a lack of moral or philosophical understanding leading to violent intent.
  • 我宁得杀耶 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Could I possibly kill?' expressing a sense of impossibility or moral restraint.
  • 我真能死汝 - Means 'I could really kill you,' emphasizing the speaker's power or capability.
  • 但恕其顽愚不足问耳 - Means 'but forgive their stubborn foolishness, they are not worth questioning,' suggesting leniency due to their lack of intelligence.
  • 使人取一犬来置书生前 - Means 'have someone bring a dog and place it in front of the scholar,' a command to prepare a demonstration.
  • 视我能杀犬否 - Means 'see if I can kill the dog,' a challenge or demonstration of power.
  • 犬适至头已堕地腹已破 - Means 'the dog arrived just as its head fell to the ground and its belly was torn open,' describing a sudden and violent event.
  • 乃叱书生曰 - Means 'then he shouted at the scholar saying,' indicating a direct and forceful command.
  • 我能使卿如犬行矣 - Means 'I can make you walk like a dog,' a threat or demonstration of control.
  • 书生下地叩头乃止 - Means 'the scholar got down on the ground and kowtowed until it stopped,' indicating submission to stop the threat.
  • 遂赦之 - Means 'so he pardoned him,' indicating forgiveness after the demonstration.
  • 仲甫有相识人居相去五百余里 - Means 'Zhongfu has acquaintances living more than five hundred miles apart,' indicating a wide geographical spread.
  • 常以张罗自业 - Means 'often engaged in setting up nets as their occupation,' describing a common activity.
  • 一旦张罗得一鸟视之乃仲甫也 - Means 'one day when they set up their nets and caught a bird, upon seeing it, it was Zhongfu,' a surprising discovery.
  • 语毕别去 - Means 'after speaking, they parted ways,' indicating the end of an interaction.
  • 是日仲甫已复至家 - Means 'that day, Zhongfu had already returned home,' indicating a quick return.
  • 在民间三百余年后入西岳山去不复还也 - Means 'after living among the people for more than three hundred years, he entered the Western Mountain and never returned,' indicating a long period of life followed by a mysterious disappearance.
  • 【出神仙传】 - Means 'from the *Book of Immortals*,' indicating the source of the story.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 汝辈愚人 - A term used to address a group of people considered unintelligent or lacking in knowledge.
  • 道学未得而欲杀之 - Refers to the concept of the Way (道) in Confucian philosophy, where moral and ethical conduct is emphasized.
  • 李意期 - A historical figure mentioned in the *Book of Immortals*, known for his supernatural abilities and interactions with immortals.
  • 汉文帝时人也 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Wen of Han, a period known for its relative peace and prosperity.
  • 无妻息 - Means 'no wife or children,' indicating a solitary life.
  • 符与之 - Means 'a talisman given to them,' indicating a magical object used for rapid travel.
  • 丹书两腋下 - Means 'a red book written on both armpits,' a magical inscription believed to aid in rapid travel.
  • 千里皆不尽日而还 - Means 'traveling a thousand miles and returning within a day,' indicating the power of the talisman.
  • 伐吴报关侯之死 - Means 'to attack Wu to avenge the death of Guan Yu,' referring to a historical event where Liu Bei sought revenge for the death of his ally Guan Yu.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 汝辈愚人道学未得而欲杀之我宁得杀耶 - This sentence is a complex rhetorical question, combining a direct address, a moral critique, and a self-reflection on the speaker's capabilities.
  • 我真能死汝但恕其顽愚不足问耳 - This sentence combines a threat with a plea for leniency, indicating a nuanced interaction.
  • 使人取一犬来置书生前曰视我能杀犬否 - This sentence is a command followed by a challenge, indicating a demonstration of power.
  • 犬适至头已堕地腹已破乃叱书生曰 - This sentence describes a sudden and violent event followed by a direct command, indicating a dramatic and forceful interaction.
  • 仲甫有相识人居相去五百余里常以张罗自业 - This sentence describes a wide geographical spread and a common activity, indicating a diverse and practical lifestyle.
  • 一旦张罗得一鸟视之乃仲甫也 - This sentence describes a surprising discovery, indicating a moment of revelation.
  • 语毕别去是日仲甫已复至家在民间三百余年后入西岳山去不复还也 - This sentence describes a sequence of events, including a quick return and a long period of life followed by a mysterious disappearance.

  • 李意期 - A historical figure mentioned in the *Book of Immortals*.
  • 汉文帝 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Han.
  • 关侯 - Refers to Guan Yu, a historical figure known for his loyalty and martial prowess.
  • 西岳山 - Refers to the Western Mountain, a location associated with immortality and supernatural events.

  • The sentence '意期不答而求纸画作兵马器仗十数万乃一一裂壊之' is elliptical and could be interpreted as '意期 did not answer but instead asked for paper to draw tens of thousands of troops and military equipment, and then tore them all apart.'
  • The phrase '一大人掘地埋之乃径还去' is ambiguous. It could mean 'a large person dug the ground and buried something, then left directly,' or 'a large person dug the ground and buried something, and then left directly.'
  • The sentence '蜀人有忧患往问之凶吉自有常候' is elliptical and could be interpreted as 'people from Shu who had troubles would ask him about fortune or misfortune, and there were always certain signs.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sentence '兵从头刺杀先造意者凭言絶而众兵一时顿地反手背上不能复动张口短气欲死其中首帅三人即鼻中出血头裂而死余者或能语曰乞放余生改恶为善' describes a series of actions and reactions, including the killing of the initial instigator, the soldiers falling to the ground, and the leaders dying. The sentence is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.
  • The long sentence '诸客或斫杀者凭禁止之乃责之曰本拟尽杀汝犹复不忍今赦汝犹敢为贼乎皆乞命曰便当易行不敢复尔凭乃天兵赦之遂各能奔走去' describes the interaction between the guests, the speaker, and the pardoned individuals. The sentence is also elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of actions and dialogues.
  • The long sentence '尝有居人妻病邪魅累年不愈凭乃之其家宅傍有泉水水自竭中有一蛟枯死又有古庙庙间有上常有光人止其下多遇暴死禽鸟不敢巢其枝凭乃勅之盛夏便枯死有大虵长七八丈悬其间而死后不复为患凭有' describes a series of supernatural events and the speaker's actions to resolve them. The sentence is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events and the speaker's interventions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 公語房曰 - Formal way of saying 'the official told Fang,' indicating a dialogue between two people.
  • 見我跳入壶中时卿便可效我跳自当得入 - Sequence of actions and commands that requires careful translation to maintain the flow and meaning.
  • 惟见仙宫世界楼观重门阁道 - Vivid description of a fantastical scene, requiring attention to the imagery and its significance.
  • 我仙人也昔处天曹以公事不勤见责因谪人间耳 - Statement of identity and past actions, which needs to be translated to convey the official's self-description and the reason for his current situation.
  • 卿可敎故得见我 - Conditional statement that must be rendered clearly to show the relationship between the two characters.
  • 肉人无知积罪却厚幸谬见哀悯犹入剖棺布气生枯起朽但恐臭顽弊不任驱使若见哀怜百生之厚幸也 - Complex sentence that describes the servant's humble background and his plea for mercy, requiring careful handling to preserve the emotional tone and the sequence of ideas.
  • 公后诣长房于楼上曰我有少酒相就饮之酒在楼下 - Series of actions and invitations that need to be translated to maintain the narrative flow.
  • 酒在楼下长房使人取之不能举盎至数十人莫能得 - Description of an attempt to retrieve something, which requires attention to the details and the outcome.
  • 上乃白公公乃下以一指提上与房共饮之酒器如拳许大 - Sequence of actions that must be translated to convey the official's supernatural ability and the size of the wine container.

  • 仙宫世界楼观重门阁道 - Reference to a celestial or divine realm, which requires attention to the imagery and its significance in Chinese culture.
  • 天曹 - Refers to the celestial bureaucracy, a concept in Chinese mythology and folklore.
  • 谪人间 - The act of being exiled to the mortal world, which is a common theme in Chinese mythology.
  • 肉人 - Term used to describe ordinary humans, often in contrast to supernatural beings.
  • 剖棺布气生枯起朽 - Metaphorical description of bringing life to the dead, which is a common theme in Chinese folklore and mythology.
  • 臭顽弊不任驱使 - Description of the servant's physical and moral state, which requires careful handling to preserve the emotional tone.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 飲之至暮不竭 - Literally 'drank until dusk without it being exhausted,' meaning the wine or liquid lasted all day.
  • 告長房曰 - Classical structure for reporting speech.
  • 我某日當去 - Future-tense statement.
  • 卿能去乎 - Rhetorical question to the listener.
  • 欲去之心不可復言 - The desire to leave cannot be spoken of again, suggesting it is too strong or obvious to need articulation.
  • 易耳 - It's easy, a colloquial expression.
  • 取一青竹杖 - Took a green bamboo staff, a key object in the story.
  • 戒之曰 - Warned him, saying, used to introduce a warning or instruction.
  • 卿以竹歸家便可稱病 - You can go home with the bamboo staff and then claim illness, a plan to feign sickness.
  • 置卿所卧處 - Place it where you sleep, indicating where the bamboo staff should be placed.
  • 黙然便來 - Be silent and come, an instruction to return quietly.
  • 去后家人見房已死尸在牀 - After he left, the family saw Fang's body on the bed, indicating the deception worked.
  • 乃向竹杖耳 - It was the bamboo staff, revealing the trick.
  • 乃哭泣葬之 - Then they wept and buried it, showing the family's grief.
  • 房诣公 - Fang went to the immortal, indicating a return or visit to the supernatural figure.
  • 恍惚不知何所 - Vaguely did not know where he was, suggesting confusion or a dreamlike state.
  • 留房于群虎中 - Left Fang among a group of tigers, a test of courage.
  • 虎磨牙張口欲噬房 - Tigers gnashed their teeth and opened their mouths to devour Fang, a threat.
  • 房不惧 - Fang was not afraid, showing bravery.
  • 明日又内于石室中 - The next day, he was placed in a stone chamber, another test.
  • 頭上有一方石廣數丈 - Above his head was a large flat stone several zhang wide, a dangerous situation.
  • 以茅绹懸之 - Suspended by a rope of straw, indicating the precariousness of the situation.
  • 又諸蛇來啮绳 - Then various snakes came to bite the rope, adding to the danger.
  • 绳即欲斷 - The rope was about to break, a near-death moment.
  • 而長房自若 - But Fang remained calm, showing composure.
  • 公至抚之曰 - The immortal came and patted him, saying, introducing a dialogue.
  • 子可敎矣 - You are teachable, a positive assessment.
  • 又令長房啗屎兼蛆 - Then he ordered Fang to eat excrement and maggots, a test of endurance and humility.
  • 長寸許 - About an inch long, describing the size of the maggots.
  • 异常臭恶 - Extremely foul and disgusting, emphasizing the unpleasantness.
  • 房难之 - Fang found it difficult, indicating struggle.
  • 公乃叹谢遣之曰 - The immortal then sighed and thanked him, saying, introducing a final statement.
  • 子不得仙道 - You cannot attain the Way of the Immortals, a rejection from the immortal.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 長房 - A known figure in Chinese legend, a disciple of an immortal who is often associated with the story of the bamboo staff (竹杖) and the ability to become invisible or transform.
  • 公 - A respectful term for an elder or a sage, here referring to the immortal who tests Longfang.
  • 竹杖 - The bamboo staff is a key item in the legend of Longfang, often used as a symbol of transformation or immortality.
  • 称病 - A common method in classical Chinese stories for someone to feign illness to avoid detection or to leave secretly.
  • 石室 - A stone chamber or cave, often used in Daoist or immortal legends as a place of trial or transformation.
  • 茅绹 - A rope made of straw, likely used here to emphasize fragility and the danger of the situation.
  • 蛇 - Snakes often appear in Daoist and supernatural stories as either threats or symbolic elements.
  • 啗屎兼蛆 - Eating excrement and maggots is a test of humility and endurance, common in Daoist and immortality legends to test a disciple's will.
  • 仙道 - The 'Way of the Immortals' or Daoist immortality, a central theme in Daoist tradition.
  • 恍惚不知何所 - A phrase that suggests a dreamlike or supernatural state, common in tales of immortals and spiritual journeys.

  • 長房 - A known figure in Daoist legend, often associated with the bamboo staff and the story of the immortal.
  • 公 - A respectful term for an elder or a sage, here referring to the immortal who tests Longfang.
  • 竹杖 - A key object in the story, often associated with Longfang and immortality.
  • 仙道 - The 'Way of the Immortals,' a central concept in Daoism.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 也赐子为地上主者可得夀数百嵗 - Elliptical phrase meaning 'If you are granted to be the ruler of the earth, you may live for hundreds of years.'
  • 为传封符一卷付之曰带此可主诸神 - He gave a scroll of the seal and said, 'Wear this and you can rule over all the spirits.'
  • 常称使者可以治病消灾 - Always call yourself an envoy and you can heal diseases and eliminate disasters.
  • 房骑竹杖辞去忽如睡已到家 - Elliptical phrase meaning 'Fang rode the bamboo staff, bid farewell, and suddenly felt as if he were sleeping, and upon waking, he had already arrived home.'
  • 家人谓是具述前事乃发棺视之惟一竹杖方信之 - The family thought he was a ghost and recounted the previous events. They then opened the coffin and saw only a bamboo staff, and they believed it.
  • 房所骑竹杖弃陂中视之乃青龙耳 - The bamboo staff that Fang rode was discarded in the pond, and when they looked at it, it turned out to be a green dragon.
  • 初去至归谓一日推问家人已一年矣 - When he left and returned, it was said to be one day, but when they asked the family, it had been a year.
  • 房乃行符收治病无不愈者 - Fang then performed rituals to capture spirits and heal diseases, and none of the illnesses were not cured.
  • 每与人同坐共语常呵责嗔怒问其故曰嗔耳 - Whenever he sat with others and talked, he often scolded and became angry. When asked why, he said, 'I am angry at the spirits.'

  • 少尝任州郡举孝廉除郎中 - Indicates that the subject served in various official positions, including being nominated as a filial and upright person (孝廉) and being appointed as a郎中 (a junior official).
  • 从军除驸马都尉 - Indicates that the subject also served in the military and was appointed as a驸马都尉 (a high-ranking military officer).
  • 顔色不老人怪之 - Means that despite living for 300 years, the subject's appearance did not age, which caused people to be surprised.
  • 性好清澹 - Means that the subject had a preference for simplicity and tranquility.
  • 闲居读易 - Indicates that the subject spent his time reading the *I Ching* (Book of Changes) in leisure.
  • 见比屋抱婴儿训求抱之 - This phrase describes an incident where the subject asked a neighbor to hold his infant.
  • 儿即死 - Means that the infant died immediately.
  • 不敢有悲哀之色 - Indicates that the neighbor showed no signs of grief.
  • 乃埋瘗之 - Means that the neighbor buried the infant.
  • 复思儿否 - Means 'Do you still miss the child?'
  • 小儿相命应不合成人 - This phrase suggests that the child's fate was such that it was not meant to grow up.
  • 但取之无苦 - Means 'Just take it; there is no need to worry.'
  • 故是汝本儿也 - Means 'It is indeed your original child.'
  • 儿识其母见而欣笑 - Means that the child recognized its mother and laughed happily.
  • 欲母取之抱犹疑不信 - Indicates that the mother was hesitant to believe that the child was alive.
  • 子训既去夫妇共往视所埋儿 - Means that after the subject left, the couple went to check on the buried child.

  • 諸老人須髪畢白者 - Refers to elderly people with fully white hair and beards.
  • 子訓但與之對坐共語 - Means 'Zixun simply sat opposite them and conversed,' describing a peaceful interaction.

  • 京師貴人聞之莫不虚心谒见 - Means 'the nobles in the capital heard about it and all came to pay respectful visits,' indicating widespread admiration.

  • 子訓知意謂生曰 - Means 'Zixun understood his intentions and said to him,' indicating a mutual understanding.
  • 卿非學道焉能如此 - A rhetorical question meaning 'how could you achieve this without learning the Dao,' emphasizing the importance of spiritual practice.
  • 尚讳之 - Means 'still conceals it,' indicating secrecy or reluctance to disclose.
  • 吾已具知卿意 - Means 'I have already fully understood your intentions,' showing Zixun's insight.
  • 諸貴人欲見我 - Means 'the nobles want to see me,' indicating their interest in Zixun.
  • 我豈以一行之勞而使卿不獲榮位乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'would I cause you to lose a prestigious position for a single visit,' emphasizing the value of the relationship.
  • 汝可還京 - Means 'you can return to the capital,' giving instructions.
  • 生父母來詣子訓 - Means 'the young man's parents came to Zixun,' showing their involvement.

  • 京師貴人 - Refers to the nobility and influential figures in the capital, indicating the social status and power of the individuals involved.

  • 子訓 - Likely refers to a historical or legendary figure known for his wisdom and spiritual abilities, though the specific identity is not clear from the text.
  • 宿昔之間明旦皆黒矣 - Suggests a supernatural or magical transformation, which is a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature.

  • 京師 - The capital city, likely referring to Chang'an or Luoyang during the Han dynasty.
  • 太学生 - Students at the Imperial College, a prestigious institution for higher education in ancient China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汝恐吾忘使汝兒失信不仕邪 - A question asking if the listener is worried that the speaker might forget to make the listener's son lose credibility by not taking office.
  • 吾今食后即发半日乃行二千里 - A statement indicating that the speaker will depart immediately after eating and will travel two thousand li in half a day.

  • 欲见者语之令各绝宾客吾明日当各诣宅 - An instruction to tell those who want to see him to dismiss their guests, as the speaker will visit each of their homes the next day.

When the speaker arrived, twenty-three people saw him simultaneously, dressed and looking the same, but their words varied according to the host's preferences.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '苏仙公者桂阳人也汉文帝时得道先生早防所怙乡中以仁孝闻宅在郡城东北出入往来不避燥湿至于食物不惮精麤' can be broken down into several parts for clarity: '苏仙公者桂阳人也' - Su Xian Gong was a person from Guiyang; '汉文帝时得道先生' - During the reign of Emperor Wen of Han, he became a person who had attained the Dao; '早防所怙' - He was early orphaned and dependent; '乡中以仁孝闻' - He was known in the village for his filial piety and benevolence; '宅在郡城东北' - His house was located in the northeast of the county town; '出入往来不避燥湿' - He traveled regardless of weather conditions; '至于食物不惮精麙' - He ate whatever was available without discrimination.
  • The sentence '先生家贫常自牧牛与里中小儿更日为牛郎先生牧之牛则徘佪侧近不驱自归余小儿牧牛牛则四散跨冈越崄' describes the daily activities and interactions of Su Xian Gong: '先生家贫常自牧牛' - Su Xian Gong, being poor, often herded the cattle himself; '与里中小儿更日为牛郎' - He took turns with the village children to be the cowherd; '先生牧之牛则徘佪侧近不驱自归' - When he herded the cattle, they would wander nearby and return on their own without being driven; '余小儿牧牛牛则四散跨冈越崄' - When the other children herded the cattle, the cattle would scatter and cross dangerous terrain.
  • The sentence '诸儿问曰尔何术也先生曰非汝辈所知常乗一鹿' describes a conversation between the children and Su Xian Gong: '诸儿问曰尔何术也' - The children asked, 'What is your secret?'; '先生曰非汝辈所知' - Su Xian Gong replied, 'It is not something you can understand'; '常乗一鹿' - He often rode a deer.
  • The sentence '先生常与母共食母曰食无鲊他日可往市买也先生于是以筯揷饭中防钱而去斯须即以鲊至母食去毕母曰何处买来对曰便县市也母曰便县去此百二十里道途径崄往来遽至汝欺我也欲杖之先生跪曰买鲊之时见舅在市与我语' describes a specific incident involving Su Xian Gong and his mother: '先生常与母共食' - Su Xian Gong often ate with his mother; '母曰食无鲊他日可往市买也' - His mother said, 'There is no preserved fish or meat; you can go to the market to buy some another day'; '先生于是以筯揷饭中防钱而去' - Su Xian Gong then inserted a chopstick into the rice and placed some coins on it and left; '斯须即以鲊至' - Shortly after, he brought back the preserved fish or meat; '母食去毕母曰何处买来对曰便县市也' - After eating, his mother asked where he bought it from, and he replied, 'From the market of the nearby county'; '母曰便县去此百二十里道途径崄往来遽至汝欺我也欲杖之' - His mother said, 'The nearby county is 120 li away, and the road is dangerous and difficult. How did you get there so quickly? You are lying to me. I want to beat you'; '先生跪曰买鲊之时见舅在市与我语' - Su Xian Gong knelt down and said, 'When I bought the preserved fish or meat, I saw my uncle in the market talking to me'.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • Long sentences like '見誠恐驚怪固請不已即出半面示一手皆有細毛異常人也' must be read as continuous narratives, describing a series of actions and a supernatural appearance.
  • Sentences like '因請郡守鄉人曰逺勞見慰途徑險阻可從直路而還不須回顾' must be read as coherent statements, introducing a request and a set of instructions.
  • Sentences like '言畢即見橋亘嶺傍直至郡城行次有一官吏輒回顾遂失橋所墮落江濱乃見一赤龍於腳下宛轉而去' must be read as continuous narratives, describing a series of events, including the appearance of a bridge, a fall, and the appearance of a red dragon.
  • Sentences like '先生哭處有桂竹兩枝無風自掃其地恒淨三年之後無復哭聲' must be read as continuous narratives, describing a miraculous event that occurs over time.
  • Sentences like '因見白馬常在嶺上遂改牛脾山為白馬嶺自後有白來止郡城東北樓上人或挾彈彈之以爪攫樓板似漆書云城郭是人民非三百甲子一來歸吾是蘇君彈何為至今修道之人每至甲子日焚香禮於仙公之故第也' must be read as continuous narratives, describing a series of events, including the renaming of a mountain, the appearance of a white horse, and a prophetic statement.

  • 周识博闻 - Profound knowledge and broad learning, describing someone with extensive intellectual capabilities.
  • 有出世之道 - Has the way to transcend the world, referring to exceptional wisdom or insight beyond ordinary understanding.
  • 鉴天文地理 - To observe celestial phenomena and terrestrial geography, indicating the ability to interpret natural signs.
  • 爻象防纬 - Divination symbols and their interpretations, a reference to the *Yijing* (Book of Changes) and its complex symbolism.
  • 安墓卜宅 - To choose burial sites and select homes, practices involving geomancy and feng shui.
  • 受其成旨 - To receive his complete instructions, suggesting that Wang Dao accepted De Guo's guidance without question.
  • 建国社稷 - To establish the state and its institutions, referring to the founding of a nation.
  • 尽规矩制度 - To follow all rules, regulations, and systems, emphasizing adherence to established norms.
  • 仰范太微星辰 - To look up to the patterns of the stars in the Big Dipper, a metaphor for following celestial guidance.
  • 俯则河洛黄图 - To follow the principles of the Yellow River and the Luo River, referring to the legendary origins of Chinese civilization.
  • 天人之助 - Divine assistance, indicating support from both heavenly and earthly forces.
  • 谋大逆 - To plot rebellion, a serious crime against the state.
  • 单骑微行 - To travel incognito on horseback, a secretive and cautious method of movement.
  • 直入姑孰城 - To enter the city of Gushu directly, indicating a bold and unexpected action.
  • 向见日月星辰之精灵 - Just now I saw the spirits of the sun, moon, and stars, a reference to supernatural beings.
  • 五岳四海之神祗 - The deities of the Five Sacred Mountains and the Four Seas, representing the pantheon of Chinese gods.
  • 导从翌卫 - Guiding and protecting, describing the entourage of divine beings.
  • 震悸失守 - Feeling shaken and losing composure, indicating a state of fear or panic.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 郭璞 - A famous scholar, geographer, and diviner of the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his expertise in feng shui and divination.
  • 王导 - A prominent statesman and advisor to the Eastern Jin dynasty, instrumental in establishing the new regime.
  • 王敦 - A powerful military leader and relative of Wang Dao, who later rebelled against the government.
  • 林明道 - Another scholar and historian, known for his writings on the same period.
  • 太微星辰 - Refers to the Big Dipper, a constellation used in ancient Chinese astronomy for navigation and divination.
  • 河洛黄图 - Legends about the origins of Chinese civilization, often associated with the *Yijing* and feng shui.
  • 五岳 - The five most sacred mountains in China: Mount Tai, Mount Hua, Mount Heng, Mount Song, and Mount Heng.
  • 四海 - Symbolic representations of the four cardinal directions, often associated with the four seas in Chinese cosmology.
  • 社稷 - Refers to the ancestral temple and the altar of the land, symbols of the state and its institutions.
  • 谋大逆 - A serious crime in ancient China, involving plotting rebellion against the emperor or the state.

Proper Nouns

  • 郭璞 - A famous scholar, geographer, and diviner.
  • 王导 - A prominent statesman and advisor.
  • 王敦 - A powerful military leader and rebel.
  • 林明道 - Another historian and scholar.
  • 太微星辰 - The Big Dipper constellation.
  • 河洛黄图 - Legends about the origins of Chinese civilization.
  • 五岳 - The five most sacred mountains in China.
  • 四海 - Symbolic representations of the four cardinal directions.
  • 社稷 - Symbols of the state and its institutions.
  • 谋大逆 - A serious crime involving rebellion.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 军敦使闻谓是小奚戏马检定非也 - A phrase where '军敦使' (jun dun shi) refers to a military envoy, '闻谓' (wen wei) means 'heard and said,' '小奚' (xiao xi) refers to a child or servant, '戏马' (xi ma) means 'play with horses,' and '检定非也' (jian ding fei ye) means 'the inspection is not correct.' This phrase is a bit ambiguous and could be interpreted as the envoy hearing that a child is playing with horses and stating that the inspection is incorrect.
  • 遣三十骑追不及 - '遣' (qian) means 'send,' '三十骑' (san shi qi) means 'thirty riders,' and '追不及' (zhui bu ji) means 'could not catch up.' This phrase indicates that the envoy sent thirty riders but they could not catch up.
  • 卿命尽防何 - '卿' (qing) means 'you,' '命' (ming) means 'fate' or 'life,' '尽' (jin) means 'end,' '防何' (fang he) is a bit ambiguous and could mean 'what to do' or 'what to prevent.' This phrase is a question about what to do or prevent when one's life is ending.
  • 下官命尽今日 - '下官' (xia guan) means 'official,' '命' (ming) means 'fate' or 'life,' '尽' (jin) means 'end,' '今日' (jin ri) means 'today.' This phrase indicates that the official's life will end today.
  • 敦诛璞江水暴上市璞尸出城南坑见璞家载棺器及送终之具已在坑侧两松间上有鹊巢璞逆报家书所言也 - '敦' (dun) refers to a person, '诛' (zhu) means 'kill,' '璞' (pu) refers to another person, '江水暴上' (jiang shui bao shang) means 'the river water rises suddenly,' '市璞尸' (shi pu shi) means 'the body of Pu is washed up,' '出城南坑' (chu cheng nan keng) means 'in the pit south of the city,' '见璞家载棺器及送终之具' (jian pu jia zai guan qi ji song zhong zhi ju) means 'seeing Pu's family carrying coffin and funeral items,' '已在坑侧两松间上有鹊巢' (yi zai keng ce liang song ji jian shang you que chao) means 'already in the pit side between two pine trees with a magpie nest on top,' '璞逆报家书所言也' (pu ni bao jia shu suo yan ye) means 'Pu's premonition in the letter he sent home.' This phrase describes a series of events where Pu's body is washed up by the river, found in a pit, and his family is seen preparing for his funeral.
  • 谓伍伯曰吾年十三时于栅塘脱袍与汝吾命应在汝手中可用吾刀伍伯感昔念惠衔涕行法殡后三日南州市人见璞货其平生服饰与相识共语非但一人敦不信开棺无尸璞得兵之道今为水仙伯注山海经夏小正尔雅方言着游仙诗江赋卜繇客傲洞林云晋书有传【出神仙传】 - '谓伍伯曰' (wei wu bo shi) means 'told Wu Bo,' '吾年十三时' (wu nian shi san shi) means 'when I was thirteen years old,' '于栅塘脱袍与汝' (yu zha tang tuo pao yu ru) means 'at Zha Tang I took off my coat and gave it to you,' '吾命应在汝手中' (wu ming ying zai ru shou zhong) means 'my fate should be in your hands,' '可用吾刀' (ke yong wu dao) means 'you can use my knife,' '伍伯感昔念惠衔涕行法' (wu bo gan xi nian hui xian ti xing fa) means 'Wu Bo was moved by the past kindness and tears, and carried out the law,' '殡后三日' (bin hou san ri) means 'three days after the burial,' '南州市人见璞货其平生服饰' (nan cheng shi ren jian pu huo qi ping sheng fu shi) means 'people in the southern market saw Pu selling his lifetime clothes,' '与相识共语非但一人' (yu xiang shi gong yu fei dan yi ren) means 'speaking with acquaintances, not just one person,' '敦不信开棺无尸' (dun bu xin kai guan wu shi) means 'Dun did not believe and opened the coffin, finding no body,' '璞得兵之道' (pu de bing zhi zhi dao) means 'Pu obtained the way of war,' '今为水仙伯' (jin wei shui xian bo) means 'now he is a water deity,' '注山海经夏小正尔雅方言着游仙诗江赋卜繇客傲洞林云晋书有传' (zhu shan hai jing xia xiao zheng er ya fang yan zhuo you xian shi jiang fu bu you ke ao dong lin yun jin shu you chuan) means 'annotating Shan Hai Jing, Xia Xiao Zheng, Er Ya, Fang Yan, writing You Xian Shi, Jiang Fu, Bu You, Ke Ao, Dong Lin, and mentioned in Jin Shu,' '【出神仙传】' (chu shen xian zhuan) means 'from the book of immortals.' This phrase describes a series of events involving Wu Bo, Pu, and the transformation of Pu into a water deity, with references to various texts and the book of immortals.
  • 尹思者字小龙安定人也晋元康五年正月十五夜坐屋中遣儿视月中有异物否儿曰今年当大水中有一人蓑带劒思目视之曰有乱卒至儿曰何以知之曰月中人乃带甲仗矛当大乱三十年复当小清耳后果如其言【出神仙传】 - '尹思者字小龙安定人也' (yin si zhe zi xiao long an ding ren ye) means 'Yin Si, also known as Xiao Long, was from An Ding,' '晋元康五年正月十五夜' (jin yuan kang wu nian zheng yue shi wu ye) means 'in the fifth year of Yuan Kang period of the Jin dynasty, on the fifteenth day of the first lunar month,' '坐屋中遣儿视月中有异物否' (zuo wu zhong qian er shi yue zhong you yi wu fou) means 'sitting in the house, he sent his son to look at the moon to see if there was anything unusual,' '儿曰今年当大水中有一人蓑带劒' (er shi jin nian dang da shui zhong you yi ren su su dai jian) means 'the son said there will be a great flood this year, and there is a person wearing a raincoat and carrying a sword in the water,' '思目视之曰有乱卒至' (si mu shi zhi shi yu you luan zu zhi) means 'Yin Si looked at it and said there will be a sudden chaos,' '儿曰何以知之曰月中人乃带甲仗矛当大乱三十年复当小清耳' (er shi he yi zhi shi yue zhong ren nai dai jia zhang mao dang da luan san shi nian fu dang xiao qing er) means 'the son said how can you know? Yin Si said the person in the moon is wearing armor and carrying a spear, there will be a great chaos, and in thirty years, there will be a small clarity.' '后果如其言' (hou guo ru qi yan) means 'as it turned out, it was as he said.' '【出神仙传】' (chu shen xian zhuan) means 'from the book of immortals.' This phrase describes a series of events involving Yin Si and his son, predicting a great flood and chaos, with references to the book of immortals.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 太平广记 - A collection of Chinese legends, anecdotes, and stories compiled during the Song Dynasty.
  • 钦定四库全书 - The 'Imperially Approved Four Treasuries,' a massive collection of Chinese literature compiled under the Qing Dynasty.
  • 神仙十四 - The fourteenth chapter of the 'Taiping Guangji' dedicated to immortals.
  • 辟疾病疫气百虎狼虺蛇蜂虿诸毒 - A phrase listing various ailments, epidemics, ghosts, tigers, wolves, snakes, scorpions, and other poisons that the pill is said to protect against.
  • 五兵白刃贼盗凶害 - Refers to weapons, sharp blades, thieves, robbers, and other dangers.
  • 雄黄各二两 - 'Two liang of realgar,' where 'liang' is an ancient unit of weight.
  • 萤火箭蒺藜各一两 - 'One liang each of fireflies, ghost arrows, and cocklebur.'
  • 铁槌柄烧令焦黒锻灶中灰羖羊角各一分半研如粉 - 'One fen and a half each of iron mallet handles, burned until black, furnace ash, and goat horns, ground into powder.'
  • 鸡子黄并丹雄鸡冠血丸如杏仁大者 - 'Egg yolk mixed with cinnabar and the blood from the comb of a rooster, made into pills the size of apricot kernels.'
  • 三角綘囊盛五丸常带左臂上 - 'Three-cornered red pouch containing five pills, to be worn on the left arm.'
  • 系腰中 - 'To be tied around the waist.'
  • 悬戸上 - 'To be hung on the door.'
  • 辟盗贼诸毒物 - 'To ward off thieves and various poisons.'
  • 汉末青牛道士封君逹得之以传安定皇甫隆隆授魏武帝 - 'At the end of the Han Dynasty, the Taoist Master Feng Jun Da obtained it and passed it on to Huang Fu Long of An Ding, who then passed it on to Emperor Wei Wu.'
  • 冠军丸 - Another name for the pill, meaning 'Champion Pill.'
  • 武威丸 - Another name for the pill, meaning 'Wuwei Pill.'
  • 千金翼中 - Refers to the 'Qian Jin Yi Fang,' a medical text compiled by Sun Simiao.

  • 永平十二年 - The 12th year of Emperor Ming of Han's reign (AD 69).
  • 武威 - An ancient Chinese city, now part of Gansu Province.
  • 青牛道士 - A Taoist term for a hermit or wandering Taoist.
  • 封君逹 - A Taoist master who lived at the end of the Han Dynasty.
  • 皇甫隆 - A Taoist master from An Ding.
  • 魏武帝 - Emperor Wei Wu, also known as Cao Cao, a prominent warlord and statesman during the late Han Dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 晦迹潜修 - To hide one's tracks and engage in quiet cultivation; refers to living a secluded and reclusive life dedicated to spiritual or intellectual pursuits.
  • 世所不知 - Unknown to the world; indicates that the person's activities or existence were not widely recognized.
  • 若有所告 - As if conveying a message; suggests that the tiger was communicating something to the person.
  • 去之 - To remove it; refers to the act of taking the bone out of the tiger's throat.
  • 驯扰 - Tame and docile; describes the tiger's behavior after the incident.
  • 抚而牵之 - To stroke and lead it; indicates the ease with which the person could interact with the tiger.
  • 随焉 - To follow; describes the tiger's behavior when the person left the mountain.
  • 俯首随行 - To bow their head and follow; emphasizes the tiger's obedience in public settings.
  • 肆暴 - To act violently; contrasts the tiger's behavior in public with its usual wild nature.
  • 如犬羊耳 - Like a dog or sheep; compares the tiger's docile behavior to that of domesticated animals.
  • 书策 - Refers to written documents or texts.
  • 背 - Refers to the back.
  • 抚我则后 - If they treat me kindly, then I will follow; indicates a reciprocal relationship.
  • 虐我则讐 - If they mistreat me, then I will become an enemy; indicates a retaliatory response.
  • 理民与驯虎亦何异哉 - Governing people and taming tigers are not very different; draws a parallel between governance and animal training.
  • 拜官不就 - Refused the official position; indicates that the person declined an offer of government service.
  • 得道而去 - Attained enlightenment and departed; suggests that the person achieved spiritual enlightenment and left the world.
  • 蜕如蛇也 - Shed its skin like a snake; refers to the process of transformation or enlightenment.

  • 天柱山 - Refers to Tianzhu Mountain, a famous mountain in China.
  • 大璧岩 - Refers to a specific location within Tianzhu Mountain.
  • 太和真人 - Refers to a Daoist deity or sage, likely a figure in Daoist mythology.
  • 冲真之道 - Refers to a Daoist path or philosophy, emphasizing purity and truth.
  • 晋书 - Refers to the *Book of Jin*, a historical text documenting the Jin Dynasty.
  • 鳌亭山 - Refers to Ao Ting Mountain, another location in the story.
  • 谶词 - Refers to prophetic or mystical sayings, often associated with Daoist or esoteric traditions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 嵩山叟 - A classical term for an old man from Mount Song, likely referring to a hermit or sage.
  • 莫测其深浅 - A phrase meaning 'impossible to measure its depth,' used to describe the vastness or mystery of the cave.
  • 同辈冀其傥不死 - A phrase meaning 'the companions hope that he might not die,' expressing the hope for survival.

  • 忽旷然见明 - A phrase meaning 'suddenly seeing light,' indicating a moment of realization or escape.
  • 中有二仙对碁 - A phrase meaning 'two immortals playing chess,' indicating a scene of divine or supernatural beings.
  • 必投身井中自当得出 - A phrase meaning 'you must throw yourself into the well and you will naturally come out,' indicating a magical or mystical solution.
  • 遂寻洞却往不知所之 - A phrase meaning 'then he searched for the cave but did not know where it was,' indicating a loss of the way back.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 于生门命轮齐于日角血脉未减心影不偏 - Highly condensed and poetic phrase referring to a person’s physical and spiritual constitution being in harmony; '日角' may refer to a facial feature or symbolic representation of the sun, and '血脉未减' and '心影不偏' indicate unimpaired vitality and balanced spirit.
  • 性贤而好法神勇而乐至 - Describes a person’s character: '性贤' (virtuous nature), '好法' (loves the law or discipline), '神勇' (divine courage), and '乐至' (delighted to the extreme). The structure is a series of parallel adjectives and adverbs common in classical Chinese.
  • 真是吾弟子也 - Direct statement meaning 'Truly, he is my disciple.' The use of '真' (truly) adds emphasis.
  • 然四十五当有大厄 - '然' introduces a contrast or concession, '四十五' is age 45, and '当有大厄' means 'will face a great calamity.' This is a common structure in classical Chinese for indicating a future misfortune.
  • 出丹书符一通贯于杖端 - '出丹书符' means 'to produce a vermilion script talisman,' and '贯于杖端' means 'fastened to the end of a staff.' This is a technical expression related to Taoist or mystical practices.
  • 令筌跪而吞之 - '令' means 'to command,' '跪' is to kneel, '吞之' is to swallow. The phrase describes a ritualistic act of ingestion, possibly in a spiritual or magical context.
  • 天地相保 - Phrase meaning 'heavens and earth will protect' or 'the heavens and earth will safeguard.' It is an idiomatic expression used in talismans or spells to invoke divine protection.
  • 此符凡三百言 - '此符' is 'this talisman,' '凡' is a classifier, and '三百言' is 'three hundred words.' This is a precise count of the text’s length.
  • 一百言演道一百言演术一百言演法 - Parallel structure: 100 words on Dao (the Way), 100 words on technique (or skill), and 100 words on law (or method). This structure is common in classical Chinese to categorize content.
  • 上有神仙抱一之道 - '上' refers to the highest level or the beginning, '神仙抱一' is a Taoist concept meaning 'immortals embracing unity,' and '之道' is 'the Way.' This is a technical expression of Taoist philosophy.
  • 中有富国安民之法 - '中' refers to the middle level or the practical level, and '富国安民之法' is 'the methods for enriching the state and pacifying the people.' This is a political or governance-related expression.
  • 下有强兵战胜之术 - '下' refers to the lower level or the tactical level, and '强兵战胜之术' is 'the techniques for strengthening the army and achieving victory in battle.' This is a military or strategic expression.
  • 皆内出心机外合人事 - '内出心机' means 'originating from the inner mind or intuition,' and '外合人事' means 'aligned with human affairs or external circumstances.' This is a philosophical statement about the relationship between internal insight and external action.
  • 其至要经传子史不足以为文 - '其至要' is 'its most essential part,' '经传子史' refers to the canonical texts of Confucianism and historiography, and '不足以为文' is 'not sufficient to serve as a model for writing.'
  • 非有道之士不可使闻之 - Statement meaning 'Only those with the Way (i.e., enlightened or spiritually advanced individuals) may be allowed to hear it.' This is a common expression in Daoist and esoteric traditions.
  • 故至人用之得其道 - '至人' is 'the perfected person' or 'the sage,' and '用之得其道' is 'uses it and attains the Way.'
  • 君子用之得 - '君子' is 'the virtuous person' or 'the gentleman,' and '用之得' is 'uses it and achieves success.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 于生 - Likely refers to a person named 'Yu' or 'Yu Sheng' (于生), possibly a disciple or student. The name is not a well-known historical figure, so it should be retained as is.
  • 日角 - A term from Chinese physiognomy, referring to a facial feature (a high, prominent forehead or a feature resembling the sun). It is associated with destiny, power, or spiritual qualities.
  • 丹书符 - A *dan shu fu* (vermilion script talisman), a key element in Taoist and alchemical practices. These were used in rituals to invoke supernatural forces or protection.
  • 黄庭内景 - *Huangting Neijing* (Yellow Court Internal Landscape), a Daoist text on internal alchemy and the cultivation of qi (vital energy). It is one of the core texts of Daoist internal cultivation.
  • 经传子史 - A reference to the classical Chinese literary canon: *Jing* (classics), *Chuan* (transmissions or commentaries), *Zi* (works of philosophers), and *Shi* (histories). These are the primary sources of Confucian and historical scholarship.
  • 孙吴韩白 - Refers to four famous Chinese generals: Sun Tzu (author of *The Art of War*), Wu Qi (military strategist), Han Xin (military general of the Han dynasty), and Bai Qi (military general of the Warring States period). These names are used as exemplars of military skill.
  • 至人 - A Daoist term meaning 'the perfected person' or 'the sage,' someone who has attained the Dao and lives in harmony with the natural order.
  • 君子 - 'The gentleman' or 'the virtuous person,' a Confucian term for a morally upright and cultivated individual.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 其术常人用之得其殃 - If ordinary people use this method, they will suffer harm.
  • 识分不同也 - Because their understanding and capabilities differ.
  • 如传同好必清斋而授之 - When passing on this to those who share the same interest, one must first purify oneself and then impart it.
  • 有本者为师 - Those who have the root knowledge are the teachers.
  • 受书者为弟子 - Those who receive the book are the disciples.
  • 不得以富贵为重贫贱为轻 - One must not value wealth and status more than poverty and lowliness.
  • 违者夺纪二十 - Those who violate this rule will lose twenty years of their life.
  • 本命日诵七遍 - Recite it seven times on one's birth day.
  • 每年七月七日写一本藏名山石岩中 - Write a copy every year on the seventh day of the seventh month and hide it in a famous mountain or stone cave.
  • 得加算久之 - One will gain longevity in the end.
  • 母曰已晡时矣 - The mother said it was already afternoon.
  • 吾有麦饭相与为食 - I have some millet porridge to share with you.
  • 袖中出一瓠 - He took a gourd out of his sleeve.
  • 令筌谷中取水 - He instructed Quan to fetch water from the valley.
  • 水既满矣 - The water was already full.
  • 瓠忽重百余斤 - The gourd suddenly became very heavy, weighing over a hundred pounds.
  • 力不能制而沉泉中 - He could not control it and it sank into the spring.
  • 及还已失老母 - When he returned, his mother was already gone.
  • 但留麦饭数升于石上而已 - Only a few measures of millet porridge were left on the stone.
  • 有相业着中台志十卷 - He also wrote ten volumes of *Zhong Tai Zhi* on his career in divination.
  • 时为李林甫所排位不显 - At the time, he was marginalized by Li Linfu and his position was not prominent.
  • 竟入名山访道不知所终 - In the end, he entered a famous mountain to seek the Dao and his whereabouts are unknown.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 中台志 - A text related to divination or astrology, possibly referring to the *Zhong Tai Zhi* (Records of the Middle Platform), a work on divination.
  • 李林甫 - A powerful and corrupt chancellor during the Tang Dynasty, known for his political machinations.
  • 名山访道 - A common motif in Daoist literature, where practitioners seek enlightenment or immortality in remote mountains.

Proper Nouns

  • 中台志 - A text related to divination or astrology.
  • 李林甫 - A powerful and corrupt chancellor during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 名山 - A famous mountain, often associated with Daoist practices of seeking enlightenment.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 相胜亿刼已来未始暂辍也 - Complex phrase meaning 'since the beginning of countless eons, it has never ceased to alternate between life and death.'
  • 得其生者合于纯阳升于天而仙 - Means 'those who gain life ascend to the pure yang and become immortals in heaven.'
  • 常命五帝三官检制部御之律令刑章罔不明备 - Means 'they often command the Five Emperors and the Three Officials to inspect, control, and enforce laws and punishments, which are all clearly established.'
  • 然而季世之民浇伪者众淳源既散妖诈萌生 - Means 'however, in the declining age, many people are deceitful, and the pure source has been scattered, leading to the emergence of evil and deceit.'
  • 不忠于君不孝于亲违三纲五常之教自投死地 - Means 'they are disloyal to their rulers, disrespectful to their parents, and violate the teachings of the Three Bonds and Five Constants, thus throwing themselves into the realm of death.'
  • 亦戕害生民驾雨乘风因衰伺隙为种种病中伤极多 - Means 'they also harm the living people, riding the rain and wind, taking advantage of the decline, and causing various diseases, inflicting great harm.'
  • 亦有不终天年罹其夭枉者 - Means 'some people do not live out their natural lifespan and suffer premature death.'
  • 昔于杜阳宫出神呪经授真人唐 - Means 'in the past, at the Du Yang Palace, the divine spell scriptures were given to the true person Tang.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 玉皇天尊 - Refers to the Jade Emperor and the Heavenly Lord, central figures in Chinese mythology and Taoism.
  • 五帝三官 - Refers to the Five Emperors and the Three Officials, divine beings who oversee the enforcement of laws and punishments in the spiritual realm.
  • 三纲五常 - Refers to the Confucian moral principles of the Three Bonds (ruler-subject, father-son, husband-wife) and the Five Constants (benevolence, righteousness, propriety, wisdom, and faithfulness).
  • 六天故气 - Refers to spiritual forces or energies that are remnants of the six heavens, often associated with chaos or negative influences.
  • 杜阳宫 - Refers to the Du Yang Palace, a mythical or sacred location where divine texts or rituals were performed.
  • 真人唐 - Refers to a 'true person' named Tang, likely a spiritual or legendary figure who received divine knowledge.

  • 母初娠梦青龙出怀并二天人降手执香炉 - Describes a dream his mother had during pregnancy, featuring a dragon and two celestial beings holding incense burners.
  • 觉语左右言当孕男子非凡人多恐无后 - Upon waking, she told those around her that she would give birth to a son who was not an ordinary person and that many feared they would not have any offspring.
  • 及生标异 - Upon birth, he was marked as extraordinary.
  • 因读神仙传有乘云驭龙之志 - After reading the 'Biographies of Immortals,' he developed a desire to ride clouds and control dragons.
  • 年二十余稍服食 - In his twenties, he began practicing alchemy.
  • 后就兴世观主孙先生咨禀经法精行道要殆通幽洞微 - Later, he sought guidance from Master Sun at Xingshi Temple, mastering the essential teachings of the Dao and nearly achieving profound insight.
  • 遂入茅山又得杨许真书遂登岩告静自称华阳隐居书疏亦以此代姓名至明 - He then entered Mount Mao, found writings by Yang and Xu, ascended a rock, and declared himself the 'Retired Scholar of Huayang,' using this name in his correspondence until his death.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 云夷甫任散诞平叔坐谭空 - Refers to Yuan Yi, a famous recluse of the Jin dynasty, and his philosophy of living a carefree life. '云' is a particle introducing a quote, '夷甫' is his courtesy name, '任散诞' means 'to take a carefree attitude,' and '平叔坐谭空' refers to his philosophical discussions.
  • 不信昭阳殿化作单于宫 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'not believing that the palace of Zhaoyang transformed into the palace of the Xiongnu king.' '昭阳殿' refers to a famous palace during the Han dynasty, and '单于宫' refers to the palace of the Xiongnu king.
  • 其时人皆谈空理不习武事 - Phrase meaning 'at that time, people were all talking about abstract theories and not practicing martial arts.' '谈空理' means 'discussing abstract or philosophical concepts,' and '不习武事' means 'not practicing martial arts.'
  • 侯景之难亦如所言 - Historical reference to the difficulties faced by the Southern Dynasties during the reign of Hou Jing, a general who rebelled against the Liang dynasty. '侯景之难' refers to the troubles caused by Hou Jing's rebellion, and '亦如所言' means 'as predicted or as said.'
  • 先生以大同二年丙辰岁三月壬寅朔十二日癸丑告化 - Historical reference to the death of a certain person, '先生,' in the year 536 AD. '大同二年丙辰岁' refers to the second year of the Da Tong era, '三月壬寅朔十二日癸丑' is the date of his death, and '告化' means 'to pass away' or 'to die.'
  • 时年八十一 - Simple phrase meaning 'he was eighty-one years old at the time.'
  • 颜色不变屈伸如常 - Phrase meaning 'his complexion did not change, and he could bend and stretch as usual.' '颜色' refers to complexion or appearance, '不变' means 'did not change,' '屈伸' means 'bending and stretching,' and '如常' means 'as usual.'
  • 室中香气积日不散 - Phrase meaning 'the fragrance in the room lingered for many days.' '室中' means 'in the room,' '香气' means 'fragrance,' '积日' means 'for many days,' and '不散' means 'did not disperse.'
  • 以其月十四日于雷平山 - Phrase meaning 'on the fourteenth day of that month, he was buried in Leiping Mountain.' '以其月' means 'on that month,' '十四日' means 'the fourteenth day,' '' is an ancient character for '葬' meaning 'to bury,' and '雷平山' refers to Leiping Mountain.
  • 同轩辕之葬衣冠如子乔之藏劒舄 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'he was buried like Emperor Huangdi, with his clothes and hats, and his sword and shoes were placed in the tomb like those of Ziqiao.' '轩辕' refers to Emperor Huangdi, '之葬' means 'his burial,' '衣冠' means 'clothes and hats,' '如子乔之藏劒舄' means 'like Ziqiao's sword and shoes being placed in the tomb,' and '子乔' is a legendary figure known for his wisdom and virtue.
  • 比于兹日可得符焉 - Phrase meaning 'on this day, one can find the symbol or sign.' '比于兹日' means 'on this day,' '可得' means 'can find,' and '符' means 'symbol' or 'sign.'
  • 诏追赠中散大夫谥贞白先生 - Phrase meaning 'an imperial decree posthumously bestowed the title of Zhong San Da Fu and the posthumous name of Zhen Bai Xian Sheng.' '诏' means 'imperial decree,' '追赠' means 'posthumously bestowed,' '中散大夫' is a high-ranking official title, '谥' means 'posthumous name,' and '贞白先生' is the posthumous name given to the person.
  • 受蓬莱都水监弟子数百人 - Phrase meaning 'he had hundreds of disciples who studied under him at the Water Commissioner of Pengle.' '受' means 'to receive' or 'to have,' '蓬莱都水监' refers to the Water Commissioner of Pengle, a position in the imperial bureaucracy, and '弟子数百人' means 'hundreds of disciples.'
  • 梁邵陵王萧纶为碑铭焉 - Phrase meaning 'the Prince of Shao Ling, Xiao Lun, wrote an inscription for the stele.' '梁邵陵王' refers to the Prince of Shao Ling, a noble title, '萧纶' is the name of the prince, '为碑铭' means 'wrote an inscription for the stele,' and '焉' is a particle used to indicate the completion of an action.
  • 出神仙感遇传 - Phrase meaning 'from the book 'Sensations and Encounters of Immortals.' '神仙' means 'immortals,' '感遇' means 'sensations and encounters,' and '传' means 'book' or 'treatise.'

  • 昭阳殿 - A famous palace during the Han dynasty, often used in literature to symbolize imperial power and luxury.

  • 侯景之难 - The difficulties faced by the Southern Dynasties during the reign of Hou Jing, a general who rebelled against the Liang dynasty.

  • 子乔 - A legendary figure known for his wisdom and virtue.
  • 中散大夫 - A high-ranking official title in the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 谥贞白先生 - The posthumous name given to the person, 'Zhen Bai Xian Sheng,' which means 'Venerable Master of Purity and Whiteness.'
  • 舍人 - An Attendant-in-Ordinary, a high-ranking official in the imperial court.
  • 蓬莱都水监 - The Water Commissioner of Pengle, a position in the imperial bureaucracy responsible for water management.
  • 梁邵陵王 - The Prince of Shao Ling, a noble title in the Liang dynasty.
  • 萧纶 - The name of the prince who wrote the inscription for the stele.
  • 神仙感遇传 - A book that records the sensations and encounters of immortals, often used in Daoist literature.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不知何许人也 - Classical phrase meaning 'whose origin is unknown.'
  • 为执役之士 - Means 'served as a servant or attendant.'
  • 性常谨黙沈静 - Describes a character as 'usually cautious, silent, and calm.'
  • 奉役之外无所营为 - Means 'beyond serving, he did nothing else,' indicating a life of simplicity and dedication to duties.
  • 自空而下集居庭中 - Describes an event where 'two green boys and white birds descended from the sky and gathered in the courtyard,' a magical or divine intervention.
  • 太上命求桓先生耳 - Means 'the Supreme One has summoned Master Huan,' indicating a divine command.
  • 修黙朝之道积年亲朝太帝 - Describes Huan Kai's practice of 'silent worship for many years, personally worshipping the Great Emperor,' a form of spiritual discipline.
  • 服天衣驾白升天而去 - Means 'dressed in heavenly robes, riding a white bird, he ascended to heaven and left,' a metaphorical description of his spiritual elevation.
  • 署蓬莱都水监 - Means 'appointed as the Water Supervisor of Penglai,' a position in the heavenly realm.
  • 言讫乃去 - Means 'after speaking, he departed,' indicating the end of his visit.
  • 草木之药可代物命者 - Refers to 'herbal medicines that can replace animal lives,' suggesting a more ethical approach to medicine.
  • 着别行本草三卷以赎其过 - Means 'wrote three volumes of a separate *Bencao* to atone for his sin,' indicating a form of penance.
  • 后果解形得道 - Means 'eventually, he transcended his physical form and attained the Dao,' a common theme in Daoist literature.
